```
1	# RFC: AGH Network v1
2	
3	- **Status:** Draft
4	- **Authors:** AGH Core Team
5	- **Created:** 2026-04-08
6	- **Depends on:** `003_agh-network-v0`
7	- **Primary addition:** `AGH Network Baseline Trust Profile`
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Abstract
12	
13	`AGH Network v1` extends v0 with cryptographic identity verification, formal conformance levels, and extension model processing. The wire format is identical to v0. A v1 peer is a v0 peer that additionally supports the Baseline Trust Profile and formal conformance claims.
14	
15	This RFC defines:
16	
17	1. `AGH Network Baseline Trust Profile` — Ed25519 signatures with JCS canonicalization
18	2. Trust state processing — `verified`, `unverified`, and `rejected` states in the processing model
19	3. Verified sender identity format — self-certified `nickname@fingerprint` handles
20	4. Proof-stripping defense — verified-format identity without proof is `rejected`
21	5. Formal conformance levels — for third-party interoperability
22	6. Extension model processing — namechanneld `ext` validation
23	7. NATS request/reply correlation
24	
25	Everything defined in v0 (envelope, message kinds, lifecycle, delivery model) remains normative. v1 extends the NATS transport with a verified-peer routing rule and a new subject prefix (`agh.network.v1`).
26	
27	---
28	
29	## 1. Scope
30	
31	This RFC does not redefine v0 semantics. It adds:
32	
33	- Section 2: Conformance levels
34	- Section 3: Trust state processing (extends v0 Section 5.2)
35	- Section 4: Baseline Trust Profile
36	- Section 5: Extension model processing
37	- Section 6: NATS additions (request/reply, fingerprint-based route tokens)
38	- Section 7: Security hardening
39	
40	For envelope format, message kinds, lifecycle, NATS transport, discovery, and delivery model, see `003_agh-network-v0`.
41	
42	---
43	
44	## 2. Conformance
45	
46	Conformance claims are additive.
47	
48	### 2.1 Core Sender
49	
50	A `Core Sender` MUST:
51	
52	- produce valid core envelopes
53	- emit valid core kinds and bodies
54	- include required lifecycle and correlation fields when applicable
55	- preserve stable sender identity formatting
56	- honor expiration semantics when it sets `expires_at`
57	
58	### 2.2 Core Receiver
59	
60	A `Core Receiver` MUST:
61	
62	- validate required envelope fields
63	- validate kind-specific payload shape
64	- honor expiration semantics
65	- tolerate duplicate delivery semantics at the application level
66	- surface trust state as `verified`, `unverified`, or `rejected`
67	- ignore unknown extension namechannels rather than failing the whole message
68	
69	### 2.3 Core Peer
70	
71	A `Core Peer` MUST satisfy both `Core Sender` and `Core Receiver`.
72	
73	### 2.4 NATS Peer
74	
75	A `NATS Peer` MUST satisfy `Core Peer` and the NATS requirements in v0 Section 10 plus the additions in this RFC Section 6.
76	
77	### 2.5 Verified Peer
78	
79	A `Verified Peer` MUST satisfy `Core Peer` and the requirements in this RFC Section 4.
80	
81	### 2.6 Reference conformance examples
82	
83	These conformance combinations are valid:
84	
85	- `Core Sender`
86	- `Core Receiver`
87	- `Core Peer`
88	- `Core Peer + NATS Peer`
89	- `Core Peer + Verified Peer`
90	- `Core Peer + NATS Peer + Verified Peer`
91	
92	---
93	
94	## 3. Trust State Processing
95	
96	v1 extends the v0 processing model (v0 Section 5.2) by inserting trust evaluation between expiration check and routing.
97	
98	### 3.1 Extended processing model
99	
100	When a receiver processes a core envelope it MUST, in this order:
101	
102	1. Validate required fields
103	2. Reject malformed messages
104	3. Evaluate expiration if `expires_at` is present
105	4. **Evaluate trust state: check `proof` if present, or check `from` format if `proof` is absent (see Section 3.3)**
106	5. Route based on `kind`, `channel`, and `to`
107	6. Apply lifecycle semantics if `interaction_id` is present
108	7. **Apply extension-specific handling only after successful core validation**
109	
110	```mermaid
111	flowchart TD
112	    Recv([Envelope received]) --> V1{Required fields present?}
113	    V1 -->|No| Reject[Reject malformed envelope]
114	    V1 -->|Yes| V2{Well-formed envelope?}
115	    V2 -->|No| Reject
116	    V2 -->|Yes| Exp{expires_at present?}
117	
118	    Exp -->|Yes| ExpCheck{Expired?}
119	    ExpCheck -->|Yes| Reject
120	    ExpCheck -->|No| Proof
121	    Exp -->|No| Proof{proof present?}
122	
123	    Proof -->|No| FromCheck{from uses nickname@fingerprint?}
124	    FromCheck -->|Yes| Rejected[Trust state = rejected]
125	    FromCheck -->|No| Unverified[Trust state = unverified]
126	    Proof -->|Yes| Profile{Supported proof profile?}
127	    Profile -->|No| Unverified
128	    Profile -->|Yes| TrustEval[Evaluate proof under profile]
129	    TrustEval --> TrustOk{Valid and allowed?}
130	    TrustOk -->|Yes| Verified[Trust state = verified]
131	    TrustOk -->|No| Rejected
132	
133	    Verified --> Route[Route by kind + channel + to]
134	    Unverified --> Route
135	    Rejected --> Stop([Reject / stop processing])
136	
137	    Route --> LC{interaction_id present?}
138	    LC -->|Yes| ApplyLC[Apply lifecycle semantics]
139	    LC -->|No| Ext[Apply extensions]
140	    ApplyLC --> Ext
141	    Ext --> Done([Done])
142	```
143	
144	### 3.2 Trust states
145	
146	The core distinguishes:
147	
148	- `verified` if proof validates under a supported trust profile
149	- `unverified` if no proof is present or the proof profile is unsupported but not malformed
150	- `rejected` if proof validation fails or policy forbids acceptance
151	
152	### 3.3 Proof-stripping defense
153	
154	If `from` uses the verified identity format (`nickname@fingerprint`) but `proof` is absent or null, the message MUST be classified as `rejected`, not `unverified`. A claimed verified-format identity without proof is treated as a failed verification, preventing proof-stripping attacks where an attacker removes `proof` from a signed message to downgrade it to `unverified` status.
155	
156	---
157	
158	## 4. AGH Network Baseline Trust Profile
159	
160	### 4.1 Profile identifier
161	
162	The baseline trust profile identifier is:
163	
164	`agh-network.trust.ed25519-jcs/v1`
165	
166	### 4.2 Purpose
167	
168	This profile guarantees verified-mode interoperability in v1 by fixing one MTI cryptographic and canonicalization scheme.
169	
170	### 4.3 MTI algorithm
171	
172	The MTI algorithm is:
173	
174	- `Ed25519` for signatures
175	- `RFC 8785 JCS` for canonical JSON serialization
176	- `SHA-256` for key fingerprint derivation
177	
178	### 4.4 Verified sender identity format
179	
180	When a peer claims this profile for verified operation, `from` MUST use:
181	
182	`nickname@fingerprint`
183	
184	Where:
185	
186	- `nickname` matches `[a-z0-9_-]{1,32}`
187	- `fingerprint` is the first 32 lowercase hexadecimal characters of `SHA-256(pubkey)`
188	
189	This preserves the self-certified handle pattern while keeping it scoped to verified-mode interoperability.
190	
191	### 4.5 Proof object
192	
193	When this profile is used, `proof` MUST have this shape:
194	
195	```json
196	{
197	  "profile": "agh-network.trust.ed25519-jcs/v1",
198	  "alg": "Ed25519",
199	  "key_id": "sha256:<64-hex>",
200	  "pubkey": "base64url(raw-32-byte-public-key)",
201	  "sig": "base64url(signature)"
202	}
203	```
204	
205	### 4.6 Signed content
206	
207	The signature covers the full envelope canonicalized with JCS, excluding only `proof.sig`.
208	
209	All other envelope fields, including the remainder of `proof`, are inside the signed content.
210	
211	### 4.7 Verification steps
212	
213	To mark a message as `verified` under this profile, a receiver MUST:
214	
215	1. confirm `proof.profile` equals `agh-network.trust.ed25519-jcs/v1`
216	2. confirm `proof.alg` equals `Ed25519`
217	3. decode `proof.pubkey`
218	4. compute `sha256(pubkey)`
219	5. confirm `proof.key_id` equals `sha256:<64-hex>`
220	6. confirm the sender fingerprint in `from` matches the first 32 lowercase hex characters of the computed digest
221	7. canonicalize the envelope with `proof.sig` omitted
222	8. verify the Ed25519 signature against the canonical bytes
223	
224	If any step fails, the message is `rejected`.
225	
226	```mermaid
227	flowchart TD
228	    Start([Envelope with proof]) --> P1{profile = ed25519-jcs/v1?}
229	    P1 -->|No| R([rejected])
230	    P1 -->|Yes| P2{alg = Ed25519?}
231	    P2 -->|No| R
232	    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Decode pubkey from base64url]
233	    P3 --> P4[Compute SHA-256 of pubkey]
234	    P4 --> P5{key_id matches sha256 digest?}
235	    P5 -->|No| R
236	    P5 -->|Yes| P6{from fingerprint matches first 32 hex?}
237	    P6 -->|No| R
238	    P6 -->|Yes| P7[JCS canonicalize envelope, omit proof.sig]
239	    P7 --> P8{Ed25519 signature valid?}
240	    P8 -->|No| R
241	    P8 -->|Yes| V([verified])
242	```
243	
244	### 4.8 Status interpretation
245	
246	Under this profile:
247	
248	- `verified` means all verification steps succeeded
249	- `unverified` means no usable baseline proof was present
250	- `rejected` means a baseline proof was present but invalid, malformed, or forbidden by local policy
251	
252	### 4.9 Verified Peer requirements
253	
254	A `Verified Peer` MUST:
255	
256	- support this baseline trust profile
257	- emit valid baseline proofs on all messages it expects peers to treat as verified
258	- reject invalid baseline proofs
259	- expose verified capability support in `Peer Card`
260	
261	---
262	
263	## 5. Extension Model Processing
264	
265	In v0, the `ext` field is active with RECOMMENDED conventions: peers MAY read and act on known keys, MUST ignore unknown keys, and the `agh.` prefix is RECOMMENDED but not enforced. In v1, extension processing is normative and namechanneld keys become a MUST requirement.
266	
267	### 5.1 Extension keys
268	
269	`ext` keys MUST be namechanneld strings. Reverse-DNS style names are RECOMMENDED, for example:
270	
271	- `io.agh.runtime`
272	- `dev.example.sandbox`
273	
274	### 5.2 Receiver behavior
275	
276	Receivers MUST ignore unknown extensions unless a higher-level profile says otherwise. Extension-specific handling MUST only be applied after successful core validation (processing model step 7).
277	
278	---
279	
280	## 6. NATS Additions
281	
282	These additions extend the v0 NATS profile (v0 Section 10).
283	
284	### 6.1 Fingerprint-based route token
285	
286	When a peer is operating in baseline verified mode and its identity is a self-certified handle (`nickname@fingerprint`), the route token MUST be the handle fingerprint suffix instead of the SHA-256 derivation.
287	
288	This means a verified peer's direct subject is:
289	
290	`agh.network.v1.<channel>.peer.<fingerprint>`
291	
292	Where `<fingerprint>` is the first 32 hex characters from the `from` field.
293	
294	### 6.2 Subject prefix
295	
296	The v1 subject prefix is:
297	
298	`agh.network.v1`
299	
300	This differs from v0's `agh.network.v0`. Peers that support both versions MUST subscribe to both prefixes.
301	
302	### 6.3 Request/reply behavior
303	
304	The profile allows use of NATS request/reply mechanics, but core semantics remain authoritative.
305	
306	If an implementation uses NATS request/reply:
307	
308	- the envelope still MUST include the correct core `reply_to`, `interaction_id`, and correlation fields
309	- NATS reply subjects do not replace core envelope correlation
310	
311	---
312	
313	## 7. Security Hardening
314	
315	These considerations extend v0 Section 11.
316	
317	### 7.1 Baseline trust profile limits
318	
319	The baseline trust profile provides message integrity and self-certified identity binding. It does not provide:
320	
321	- global trust roots
322	- revocation infrastructure
323	- organization-level authorization
324	- federation-wide policy enforcement
325	
326	Those belong in future profiles or deployment-specific policy.
327	
328	### 7.2 Proof presence does not imply validity
329	
330	Transport authentication is not assumed. Proof presence does not imply proof validity. Receivers MUST always execute the full verification steps (Section 4.7) before marking a message as `verified`.
331	
332	---
333	
334	## 8. Worked Example: Verified `say`
335	
336	This envelope shows verified-mode shape: `from` uses `nickname@fingerprint`, and `proof` matches Section 4.5.
337	
338	```json
339	{
340	  "protocol": "agh-network/v1",
341	  "id": "msg_verified_say_01",
342	  "kind": "say",
343	  "channel": "builders",
344	  "from": "patch-worker@39f713d0a644253f04529421b9f51b9b",
345	  "to": null,
346	  "interaction_id": null,
347	  "reply_to": null,
348	  "trace_id": "trace_verified_example",
349	  "causation_id": null,
350	  "ts": 1775606300,
351	  "expires_at": null,
352	  "body": {
353	    "text": "Baseline proof example only.",
354	    "artifacts": []
355	  },
356	  "proof": {
357	    "profile": "agh-network.trust.ed25519-jcs/v1",
358	    "alg": "Ed25519",
359	    "key_id": "sha256:39f713d0a644253f04529421b9f51b9b08979d08295959c4f3990ee617f5139f",
360	    "pubkey": "PUAXw-hDiVqStwqnTRt-vJyYLM8uxJaMwM1V8Sr0Zgw",
361	    "sig": "qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqg"
362	  },
363	  "ext": {}
364	}
365	```
366	
367	The `sig` field above is a length-appropriate placeholder for documentation; it will not verify until replaced by a real signature over the canonical bytes for this exact envelope (Section 4.6).
368	
369	---
370	
371	## 9. Normative References
372	
373	1. RFC 8785, JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS)
374	2. RFC 8032, Edwards-Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (EdDSA)
375	3. FIPS 180-4, Secure Hash Standard (SHA-256)
376	4. `003_agh-network-v0` (this project)
377	
378	---
379	
380	## 10. Outcome
381	
382	`AGH Network v1` adds trust and formal interoperability to the v0 foundation:
383	
384	- verified identity through Ed25519 + JCS baseline trust profile
385	- proof-stripping defense
386	- formal conformance levels for third-party implementations
387	- extension model processing
388	- NATS fingerprint-based routing for verified peers
389	
390	The wire format is identical to v0. The upgrade path is purely additive.
391	
```

1	// Package network defines the AGH Network v0 protocol surface shared by the
2	// transport, router, and delivery layers.
3	package network
4	
5	import (
6		"encoding/json"
7		"fmt"
8	)
9	
10	// ProtocolV0 is the RFC v0 wire protocol identifier.
11	const ProtocolV0 = "agh-network/v0"
12	
13	// Kind identifies one normative AGH Network message kind.
14	type Kind string
15	
16	const (
17		KindGreet      Kind = "greet"
18		KindWhois      Kind = "whois"
19		KindSay        Kind = "say"
20		KindDirect     Kind = "direct"
21		KindCapability Kind = "capability"
22		KindReceipt    Kind = "receipt"
23		KindTrace      Kind = "trace"
24	)
25	
26	var validKinds = map[Kind]struct{}{
27		KindGreet:      {},
28		KindWhois:      {},
29		KindSay:        {},
30		KindDirect:     {},
31		KindCapability: {},
32		KindReceipt:    {},
33		KindTrace:      {},
34	}
35	
36	// Validate reports whether the kind is one of the documented RFC values.
37	func (k Kind) Validate() error {
38		if _, ok := validKinds[k]; !ok {
39			return fmt.Errorf("%w: kind=%q", ErrInvalidKind, string(k))
40		}
41		return nil
42	}

46

163	type Proof map[string]json.RawMessage

166	type ExtensionMap map[string]json.RawMessage
167	
168	// Envelope is the shared AGH Network v0 wire envelope.
169	type Envelope struct {
170		Protocol      string          `json:"protocol"`
171		ID            string          `json:"id"`
172		Kind          Kind            `json:"kind"`
173		Channel       string          `json:"channel"`
174		From          string          `json:"from"`
175		To            *string         `json:"to"`
176		InteractionID *string         `json:"interaction_id,omitempty"`
177		ReplyTo       *string         `json:"reply_to,omitempty"`
178		TraceID       *string         `json:"trace_id,omitempty"`
179		CausationID   *string         `json:"causation_id,omitempty"`
180		TS            int64           `json:"ts"`
181		ExpiresAt     *int64          `json:"expires_at,omitempty"`
182		Body          json.RawMessage `json:"body"`
183		Proof         *Proof          `json:"proof"`
184		Ext           ExtensionMap    `json:"ext,omitempty"`
185	}
186	
187	// IsDirected reports whether the envelope targets a specific peer.
188	func (e Envelope) IsDirected() bool {
189		return e.To != nil
190	}
191	
192	// IsBroadcast reports whether the envelope is channel-broadcast.
193	func (e Envelope) IsBroadcast() bool {
194		return e.To == nil
195	}
196	
197	// DecodeBody parses and validates the envelope body using the envelope kind.
198	func (e Envelope) DecodeBody() (Body, error) {
199		return DecodeBody(e.Kind, e.Body)

201	
202	// Body is the typed representation of one envelope body.
203	type Body interface {
204		Kind() Kind
205	}
206

210		_ Body = SayBody{}

212		_ Body = CapabilityBody{}
213		_ Body = ReceiptBody{}
214		_ Body = TraceBody{}

242	}

246	
247	// SayBody carries broadcast chat-first communication.
248	type SayBody struct {

270	}

274	
275	// CapabilityEnvelopePayload is the transferable unified capability document.
276	type CapabilityEnvelopePayload struct {
277		ID                string   `json:"id"`
278		Summary           string   `json:"summary"`
279		Outcome           string   `json:"outcome"`
280		Version           string   `json:"version,omitempty"`
281		Digest            string   `json:"digest"`
282		ContextNeeded     []string `json:"context_needed,omitempty"`
283		ArtifactsExpected []string `json:"artifacts_expected,omitempty"`
284		ExecutionOutline  []string `json:"execution_outline,omitempty"`
285		Constraints       []string `json:"constraints,omitempty"`
286		Examples          []string `json:"examples,omitempty"`
287		Requirements      []string `json:"requirements,omitempty"`
288	}
289	
290	// ReceiptBody acknowledges or rejects protocol-level admission.
291	type ReceiptBody struct {
292		ForID      string        `json:"for_id"`
293		Status     ReceiptStatus `json:"status"`
294		ReasonCode *ReasonCode   `json:"reason_code,omitempty"`
295		Detail     *string       `json:"detail,omitempty"`
296	}
297	
298	// Kind returns the wire kind for the body.
299	func (ReceiptBody) Kind() Kind { return KindReceipt }
300	
301	// TraceBody reports progress or terminal outcome for an interaction.
302	type TraceBody struct {
303		State        InteractionState  `json:"state"`
304		Message      string            `json:"message,omitempty"`
305		Result       json.RawMessage   `json:"result,omitempty"`
306		ArtifactRefs []json.RawMessage `json:"artifact_refs,omitempty"`
307	}
308	
309	// Kind returns the wire kind for the body.
310	func (TraceBody) Kind() Kind { return KindTrace }
311	
```

```
1	# RFC: AGH Network v0
2	
3	- **Status:** Draft
4	- **Authors:** AGH Core Team
5	- **Created:** 2026-04-08
6	- **Superseded by:** `AGH Network v1` (adds trust profile and formal conformance)
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Abstract
11	
12	`AGH Network v0` is the first implementable iteration of the AGH Network protocol. It defines the full wire format, all seven message kinds, the complete interaction lifecycle, NATS transport binding, and operational delivery semantics. It does not include cryptographic identity verification, which is deferred to v1.
13	
14	v0 is designed to be wire-compatible with v1: the envelope schema is identical, and a v0 peer can receive v1 messages (treating `proof` as opaque and all identities as `unverified`). Upgrading to v1 adds trust verification without changing the wire format.
15	
16	---
17	
18	## 1. Overview
19	
20	### 1.1 Problem
21	
22	The agent ecosystem lacks a lightweight open agent network protocol that is practical to implement, transport-aware, artifact-aware, and operationally observable without collapsing into a workflow engine or telemetry infrastructure.
23	
24	### 1.2 Scope of v0
25	
26	v0 delivers:
27	
28	- the complete envelope schema (wire-compatible with v1)
29	- all seven message kinds: `greet`, `whois`, `say`, `direct`, `capability`, `receipt`, `trace`
30	- the full interaction lifecycle with all six states
31	- the normative NATS transport binding
32	- delivery semantics, error model, and reason codes
33	- minimal discovery via `greet` and `whois`
34	- Peer Card, capability discovery, and capability transfer signaling
35	
36	v0 does not deliver:
37	
38	- cryptographic identity verification (Ed25519 + JCS)
39	- the `verified` and `rejected` trust states
40	- the Baseline Trust Profile
41	- formal conformance levels for third-party interoperability
42	- normative extension namespacing (v0 uses `ext` with RECOMMENDED conventions; v1 makes namespacing MUST)
43	
44	### 1.3 Upgrade path to v1
45	
46	v0 is a proper subset of v1. The envelope schema is identical. A v0 implementation upgrades to v1 by:
47	
48	1. implementing the Baseline Trust Profile (Ed25519 + JCS signing and verification)
49	2. supporting `verified` and `rejected` trust states in the processing model
50	3. adopting the `nickname@fingerprint` identity format for verified peers
51	4. implementing proof-stripping detection
52	5. optionally claiming formal conformance levels
53	
54	No wire format changes are required.
55	
56	---
57	
58	## 2. Goals and Non-Goals
59	
60	### 2.1 Goals
61	
62	1. Define the complete wire format shared with v1
63	2. Define all seven message kinds
64	3. Define the full interaction lifecycle
65	4. Define the normative NATS transport binding
66	5. Support peer discovery through `greet` and `whois`
67	6. Support first-class `capability` transfer
68	7. Be implementable quickly by a small team
69	
70	### 2.2 Non-Goals
71	
72	1. Cryptographic identity verification
73	2. Formal conformance levels for third-party interoperability
74	3. Normative extension namespacing (RECOMMENDED conventions only)
75	4. NATS request/reply mechanics
76	5. Everything listed as non-goals in v1 (workflow engine, federation, service registry, etc.)
77	
78	---
79	
80	## 3. Terminology
81	
82	### 3.1 Peer
83	
84	A `Peer` is any implementation that can emit, receive, or both emit and receive `AGH Network` envelopes.
85	
86	### 3.2 Channel
87	
88	A `Channel` is a logical communication namechannel. Channels are protocol-visible but transport-neutral. A transport profile decides how channels map to transport primitives.
89	
90	A `channel` value MUST match `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,63}`. Characters outside this set — including dots, whitespace, and NATS wildcard tokens (`>`, `*`) — are forbidden because channel values are interpolated directly into transport subjects.
91	
92	### 3.3 Interaction
93	
94	An `Interaction` is the lightweight logical container for work or conversation progression. It is identified by `interaction_id` and may move through a small lifecycle.
95	
96	An interaction is scoped to the tuple `(channel, interaction_id)`. The same `interaction_id` string in different channels denotes different interactions. Only the two original peers — the initiator who sent the first interaction-bearing `direct` or `capability` and the target identified in `to` — MAY emit lifecycle messages (`receipt`, `trace`, `direct`, `capability`) for that interaction. Messages from other peers referencing an `interaction_id` they did not initiate or were not targeted by SHOULD be ignored.
97	
98	### 3.4 Capability
99	
100	A `Capability` is the reusable AGH delegation artifact. The same structured capability concept is used for authored catalogs, brief discovery, rich discovery, and explicit `kind:"capability"` transfer. It is intentionally interpretive, not a deterministic workflow program.
101	
102	### 3.5 Claimed Identity
103	
104	The sender identity present in the envelope.
105	
106	### 3.6 Profile
107	
108	A named extension of the core that defines transport behavior, trust mechanics, or other interoperability layers.
109	
110	---
111	
112	## 4. Architecture
113	
114	### 4.1 Layer model
115	
116	v0 defines two normative layers:
117	
118	1. `AGH Network Core`
119	2. `AGH Network over NATS`
120	
121	```mermaid
122	flowchart TD
123	    subgraph L1["AGH Network Core"]
124	        E["Envelope"]
125	        K["Message Kinds"]
126	        IL["Lifecycle"]
127	        D["Discovery"]
128	    end
129	
130	    subgraph L2["AGH Network over NATS"]
131	        SM["Subject Mapping"]
132	        SR["Sending Rules"]
133	        RP["Reliability Posture"]
134	    end
135	
136	    L1 --> L2
137	```
138	
139	### 4.2 AGH Network Core
140	
141	The core defines:
142	
143	- canonical envelope semantics
144	- message kinds
145	- capability transfer model
146	- interaction lifecycle
147	- minimal discovery and capability signaling
148	- minimal observability primitives
149	- semantic delivery rules
150	
151	The core does not define:
152	
153	- cryptographic identity verification (deferred to v1)
154	- NATS subject grammar (defined by the NATS profile)
155	- broker topology
156	- retry policy details
157	- replay backends
158	- runtime telemetry pipelines
159	- AGH daemon behavior
160	- sandbox execution or scheduling
161	
162	### 4.3 AGH Network over NATS
163	
164	The NATS profile defines:
165	
166	- subject mapping
167	- broadcast and direct routing
168	- operational behavior specific to NATS
169	- profile-specific constraints on delivery behavior
170	
171	### 4.4 Product boundary
172	
173	This RFC does not require AGH. However, AGH is expected to provide the reference Go implementation with the strongest operational observability and runtime ergonomics.
174	
175	---
176	
177	## 5. Core Protocol
178	
179	### 5.1 Envelope
180	
181	Every message is a single envelope carrying protocol semantics independent of transport. Envelopes MUST be serialized as UTF-8 JSON.
182	
183	#### 5.1.1 Canonical fields
184	
185	| Field            | Type            | Required | Notes                                          |
186	| ---------------- | --------------- | -------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
187	| `protocol`       | string          | yes      | MUST be `agh-network/v0`                       |
188	| `id`             | string          | yes      | collision-resistant message identifier         |
189	| `kind`           | string          | yes      | one of the normative kinds defined by this RFC |
190	| `channel`        | string          | yes      | logical namechannel                            |
191	| `from`           | string          | yes      | claimed sender identity                        |
192	| `to`             | string or null  | no       | target peer for directed communication         |
193	| `interaction_id` | string or null  | no       | logical interaction identifier                 |
194	| `reply_to`       | string or null  | no       | message identifier being replied to            |
195	| `trace_id`       | string or null  | no       | distributed correlation identifier             |
196	| `causation_id`   | string or null  | no       | parent causal message identifier               |
197	| `ts`             | integer         | yes      | Unix epoch seconds                             |
198	| `expires_at`     | integer or null | no       | sender-declared TTL boundary                   |
199	| `body`           | object          | yes      | kind-specific payload                          |
200	| `proof`          | object or null  | no       | reserved for v1 trust profile                  |
201	| `ext`            | object          | no       | extension map for implementation-specific data |
202	
203	#### 5.1.2 Field requirements by kind
204	
205	- `to` MUST be present for `direct`, targeted `whois`, targeted `receipt`, and targeted `trace`
206	- `interaction_id` MUST be present for `direct`, `receipt`, and `trace`
207	- `reply_to` SHOULD be present for responses and follow-up interaction messages
208	- `trace_id` SHOULD be present whenever a message belongs to a larger operational flow
209	- `causation_id` SHOULD be present when a message is causally derived from another message
210	
211	### 5.2 Processing model
212	
213	When a receiver processes a core envelope it MUST, in this order:
214	
215	1. Validate required fields
216	2. Reject malformed messages
217	3. Evaluate expiration if `expires_at` is present
218	4. Route based on `kind`, `channel`, and `to`
219	5. Apply lifecycle semantics if `interaction_id` is present
220	
221	```mermaid
222	flowchart TD
223	    Recv([Envelope received]) --> V1{Required fields present?}
224	    V1 -->|No| Reject[Reject malformed envelope]
225	    V1 -->|Yes| V2{Well-formed envelope?}
226	    V2 -->|No| Reject
227	    V2 -->|Yes| Exp{expires_at present?}
228	
229	    Exp -->|Yes| ExpCheck{Expired?}
230	    ExpCheck -->|Yes| Reject
231	    ExpCheck -->|No| Route
232	    Exp -->|No| Route[Route by kind + channel + to]
233	
234	    Route --> LC{interaction_id present?}
235	    LC -->|Yes| ApplyLC[Apply lifecycle semantics]
236	    LC -->|No| Done([Done])
237	    ApplyLC --> Done
238	```
239	
240	### 5.3 Extension model
241	
242	The `ext` field carries implementation-specific data that is not part of the core protocol semantics. Peers MAY read and act on known `ext` keys. Peers MUST ignore unknown `ext` keys.
243	
244	In v0, short-prefix namespacing is RECOMMENDED but not enforced. The `agh.` prefix is RECOMMENDED for AGH-specific keys. Examples:
245	
246	```json
247	{
248	  "ext": {
249	    "agh.session_id": "ses_ab_01",
250	    "agh.workspace": "/Users/pedro/project"
251	  }
252	}
253	```
254	
255	In v1, namechanneld keys become a normative requirement (MUST).
256	
257	### 5.4 Trust state in v0
258	
259	In v0, all messages are treated as `unverified`. The `proof` field is reserved on the wire for forward compatibility with v1 but is not processed. Receivers MUST NOT reject messages based on `proof` content in v0.
260	
261	---
262	
263	## 6. Identity, Discovery, and Capabilities
264	
265	### 6.1 Identity in v0
266	
267	The core requires a stable claimed identity in `from`. It does not require a centralized authority or registry.
268	
269	A `peer_id` value (used in `from`, `to`, and Peer Card) MUST match `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9._-]{0,127}`. This constraint ensures deterministic route token derivation across implementations.
270	
271	### 6.2 Peer Card
272	
273	`greet` and `whois` use a shared `Peer Card` object.
274	
275	#### 6.2.1 Peer Card fields
276	
277	| Field                   | Type            | Required | Notes                                                 |
278	| ----------------------- | --------------- | -------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
279	| `peer_id`               | string          | yes      | canonical peer identity                               |
280	| `display_name`          | string or null  | no       | human-friendly label                                  |
281	| `profiles_supported`    | array of string | yes      | supported protocol profiles                           |
282	| `capabilities`          | array of string | yes      | minimal capability identifiers advertised by the peer |
283	| `artifacts_supported`   | array of string | yes      | artifact types the peer understands                   |
284	| `trust_modes_supported` | array of string | yes      | for example `unverified`                              |
285	| `ext`                   | object          | no       | profile-specific or runtime-specific metadata         |
286	
287	`capabilities` is the minimal capability index advertised by the peer. Implementations MAY expose richer capability discovery metadata through `ext`.
288	
289	`artifacts_supported` advertises which transferable artifact kinds the peer understands. AGH v0 peers that support unified capability transfer SHOULD advertise `"capability"` in `artifacts_supported` even when `capabilities` is empty.
290	
291	### 6.3 Minimal discovery
292	
293	The core defines minimal discovery only:
294	
295	- `greet` for unsolicited or periodic peer advertisement
296	- `whois` for lookup and on-demand capability retrieval
297	
298	The core does not define:
299	
300	- distributed registries
301	- discovery gossip
302	- trust directories
303	- global service catalogs
304	
305	### 6.4 Capability semantics
306	
307	A capability is the single AGH delegation artifact. The same concept appears in three protocol roles:
308	
309	- discovery index in `peer_card.capabilities`
310	- optional rich discovery through explicit `whois`
311	- transferable artifact through `kind:"capability"`
312	
313	Capability IDs are peer-local stable identifiers. Slugs are RECOMMENDED, for example:
314	
315	- `collect-failing-tests`
316	- `fix-go-migration-tests`
317	- `review-runtime-docs`
318	
319	On the network, the effective operational identity is `(peer_id, capability_id)`.
320	
321	Capability advertisements are advisory. They indicate what a peer claims it can do, but they do not imply authorization, safety, or guaranteed execution semantics.
322	
323	The core does not require capabilities to correspond to tools, prompts, or deterministic workflow definitions, and it does not impose a global capability taxonomy.
324	
325	Implementations MAY expose richer capability discovery metadata using `ext`, but the steady-state model remains one capability concept rather than separate discovery and transfer artifact types.
326	
327	---
328	
329	## 7. Interaction Model and Lifecycle
330	
331	### 7.1 Interaction model
332	
333	The protocol is chat-first, but operationally useful. `Interaction` is the minimal shared abstraction between those two goals.
334	
335	An interaction:
336	
337	- is identified by `interaction_id`
338	- groups related messages
339	- can be opened by a sender through `direct` or `capability` when `interaction_id` is present
340	- can progress through a lightweight lifecycle
341	
342	### 7.2 Lifecycle states
343	
344	The normative lifecycle states are:
345	
346	- `submitted`
347	- `working`
348	- `needs_input`
349	- `completed`
350	- `failed`
351	- `canceled`
352	
353	```mermaid
354	stateDiagram-v2
355	    [*] --> submitted : first direct/capability with interaction_id opens interaction
356	
357	    submitted --> working : trace working
358	    submitted --> needs_input : trace needs_input
359	    submitted --> completed : trace completed
360	    submitted --> failed : receipt rejected or trace failed
361	    submitted --> canceled : receipt canceled or trace canceled
362	
363	    working --> working : trace working (progress)
364	    working --> needs_input : trace needs_input
365	    working --> completed : trace completed
366	    working --> failed : trace failed
367	    working --> canceled : trace canceled
368	
369	    needs_input --> working : direct reply resumes work
370	    needs_input --> completed : trace completed
371	    needs_input --> failed : trace failed
372	    needs_input --> canceled : trace canceled
373	
374	    completed --> [*]
375	    failed --> [*]
376	    canceled --> [*]
377	```
378	
379	### 7.2.1 Post-terminal behavior
380	
381	Once an interaction reaches a terminal state (`completed`, `failed`, or `canceled`), receivers MUST ignore any subsequent `trace` messages for that `interaction_id` that attempt further state transitions. A `direct` arriving after a terminal state does not reopen the interaction; the receiver MAY emit `receipt` with `status = rejected` and `reason_code = interaction_closed`.
382	
383	If out-of-order delivery causes a non-terminal `trace` (for example `working`) to arrive after a terminal `trace` (for example `completed`), the receiver MUST NOT regress the interaction state. The terminal state is authoritative.
384	
385	### 7.3 Lifecycle intent
386	
387	These states are intentionally lightweight. They exist for:
388	
389	- handoff
390	- progress tracking
391	- human-in-the-loop pauses
392	- completion and failure reporting
393	
394	They do not imply:
395	
396	- workflow graph semantics
397	- orchestration plans
398	- retries as protocol state
399	- compensation logic
400	
401	### 7.4 Lifecycle signaling
402	
403	- the opening interaction message implies `submitted`
404	- `receipt` MAY acknowledge acceptance or rejection
405	- `trace` carries `working`, `needs_input`, `completed`, `failed`, or `canceled`
406	
407	#### Cancellation semantics
408	
409	`receipt` with `status = canceled` and `trace` with `state = canceled` serve different roles:
410	
411	- `receipt(canceled)` is initiator-side cancellation — the peer that opened the interaction withdraws the request before or shortly after work begins
412	- `trace(canceled)` is worker-side cancellation — the peer performing work aborts during execution
413	
414	If both arrive for the same interaction, the first to be processed establishes the terminal state. The second MUST be ignored per Section 7.2.1.
415	
416	### 7.5 Minimal observability
417	
418	The core REQUIRES only enough observability to preserve lineage and operational context:
419	
420	- `id`
421	- `interaction_id` where applicable
422	- `reply_to`
423	- `trace_id`
424	- `causation_id`
425	- `receipt`
426	- `trace`
427	
428	The core does not define:
429	
430	- span exporters
431	- metrics schemas
432	- replay storage formats
433	- telemetry backends
434	
435	---
436	
437	## 8. Message Kinds
438	
439	### 8.1 Overview
440	
441	The normative core kinds are:
442	
443	- `greet`
444	- `whois`
445	- `say`
446	- `direct`
447	- `capability`
448	- `receipt`
449	- `trace`
450	
451	```mermaid
452	sequenceDiagram
453	    participant A as Peer A
454	    participant S as Channel
455	    participant B as Peer B
456	
457	    A->>S: greet
458	    S-->>B: greet
459	    A->>B: whois
460	    B-->>A: whois response
461	    B->>S: say
462	    S-->>A: say
463	    A->>B: direct
464	    B-->>A: receipt
465	    B-->>A: trace
466	    B->>S: capability
467	    S-->>A: capability
468	```
469	
470	### 8.2 `greet`
471	
472	`greet` advertises peer presence and capabilities to a channel.
473	
474	#### Body
475	
476	```json
477	{
478	  "peer_card": {},
479	  "summary": "optional free-form announcement"
480	}
481	```
482	
483	#### Rules
484	
485	- `peer_card` is REQUIRED
486	- `to` SHOULD be null
487	- `interaction_id` SHOULD be null
488	
489	#### AGH capability brief extension
490	
491	AGH MAY project a normalized local capability catalog into the `Peer Card` brief-discovery surface:
492	
493	- `peer_card.capabilities` remains the compact list of capability IDs
494	- `peer_card.ext["agh.capabilities_brief"]` MAY carry an array of objects with only:
495	  - `id`
496	  - `summary`
497	
498	If `agh.capabilities_brief` is present, its entries MUST align with `peer_card.capabilities` in the same order. If a peer has no local capability catalog, AGH SHOULD emit `peer_card.capabilities = []` and omit `agh.capabilities_brief`.
499	
500	Recommended shape:
501	
502	```json
503	[
504	  {
505	    "id": "create-landing-page",
506	    "summary": "Creates a landing page from a brief."
507	  }
508	]
509	```
510	
511	- summaries SHOULD remain short enough for periodic `greet` traffic
512	- summaries SHOULD be a single short sentence and SHOULD target `<= 160` UTF-8 characters in v0
513	
514	Local capability authoring, file layout, and validation are runtime concerns and are documented separately in `docs/rfcs/005_capability-catalogs-agent-directories.md`.
515	
516	### 8.3 `whois`
517	
518	`whois` retrieves or returns peer card information.
519	
520	#### Request body
521	
522	```json
523	{
524	  "type": "request",
525	  "query": "peer_id or capability query"
526	}
527	```
528	
529	#### Response body
530	
531	```json
532	{
533	  "type": "response",
534	  "peer_card": {}
535	}
536	```
537	
538	#### Rules
539	
540	- `type` is REQUIRED and MUST be either `request` or `response`
541	- a response `whois` MUST set `reply_to`
542	- targeted lookup SHOULD set `to`
543	- untargeted lookup MAY be broadcast within a channel
544	
545	#### AGH rich capability discovery extension
546	
547	AGH MAY use envelope `ext` keys to request or return a rich capability catalog without bloating ordinary `whois` traffic. `whois` remains the rich-discovery path; `kind:"capability"` remains the explicit transfer path.
548	
549	Request keys:
550	
551	- `ext["agh.include"]` MAY contain `"capability_catalog"` to request rich capability discovery
552	- `ext["agh.capability_ids"]` MAY contain a list of capability IDs used to filter the returned catalog
553	
554	Response key:
555	
556	- `ext["agh.capability_catalog"]` MAY contain:
557	
558	Recommended request shapes:
559	
560	Full catalog request:
561	
562	```json
563	{
564	  "ext": {
565	    "agh.include": ["capability_catalog"]
566	  }
567	}
568	```
569	
570	Filtered catalog request:
571	
572	```json
573	{
574	  "ext": {
575	    "agh.include": ["capability_catalog"],
576	    "agh.capability_ids": ["create-landing-page"]
577	  }
578	}
579	```
580	
581	Recommended response shape:
582	
583	```json
584	{
585	  "ext": {
586	    "agh.capability_catalog": {
587	      "capabilities": [
588	        {
589	          "id": "create-landing-page",
590	          "summary": "Creates a landing page from a brief.",
591	          "outcome": "Landing page ready or near-ready with copy, structure, and implementation or proposal.",
592	          "version": "1.2.0",
593	          "digest": "sha256:4ac7c4d8f64f35672e0e46ae7b8cfb2fd8d8a48fd6a0f4f37ab89f4459ef560f",
594	          "context_needed": ["product", "target audience", "offer"],
595	          "artifacts_expected": ["copy", "page structure", "implementation"],
596	          "execution_outline": [
597	            "understand the brief",
598	            "run discovery",
599	            "draft copy",
600	            "compose the page",
601	            "refine and deliver"
602	          ],
603	          "requirements": ["draft-offer-brief"]
604	        }
605	      ]
606	    }
607	  }
608	}
609	```
610	
611	Rules:
612	
613	- rich capability discovery is explicit; without `agh.include=["capability_catalog"]`, AGH SHOULD keep `whois` responses at the minimal `peer_card` shape
614	- the response still returns the normal `peer_card`
615	- the rich catalog belongs in envelope `ext`, not in `peer_card.ext`
616	- each capability entry MUST include `id`, `summary`, and `outcome`
617	- each capability entry MAY include `version`, `digest`, `context_needed`, `artifacts_expected`, `execution_outline`, `constraints`, `examples`, and `requirements`
618	- peers SHOULD NOT include full capability catalogs in periodic `greet`
619	- peers MAY return the full catalog or a filtered subset based on `agh.capability_ids`
620	- if the peer has no local catalog, `agh.capability_catalog.capabilities` MUST be `[]`
621	- if `agh.capability_ids` filters out every capability, `agh.capability_catalog.capabilities` MUST be `[]`
622	- requesters SHOULD prefer `agh.capability_ids` once brief discovery identifies likely matches
623	- responders MUST NOT emit a `whois` response exceeding the envelope size limit
624	- if a full rich catalog would exceed the envelope size limit, responders SHOULD omit `agh.capability_catalog` unless a narrower filtered request is provided
625	- receivers MUST ignore unknown AGH extension keys per the core `ext` rules
626	
627	The local capability catalog model and validation rules are runtime concerns and are documented separately in `docs/rfcs/005_capability-catalogs-agent-directories.md`.
628	
629	### 8.4 `say`
630	
631	`say` is chat-first, channel-scoped communication.
632	
633	#### Body
634	
635	```json
636	{
637	  "text": "message text",
638	  "artifacts": [],
639	  "intent": "optional intent label"
640	}
641	```
642	
643	#### Rules
644	
645	- `say` SHOULD be used for channel-visible communication
646	- `to` SHOULD be null
647	- `interaction_id` MAY be absent
648	
649	### 8.5 `direct`
650	
651	`direct` opens or continues a targeted interaction.
652	
653	#### Body
654	
655	```json
656	{
657	  "text": "message text",
658	  "intent": "optional intent label",
659	  "artifacts": []
660	}
661	```
662	
663	#### Rules
664	
665	- `to` is REQUIRED
666	- `interaction_id` is REQUIRED
667	- a `direct` envelope with a new `interaction_id` opens that interaction unless a prior interaction-bearing `capability` already established it
668	
669	#### Example
670	
671	```json
672	{
673	  "protocol": "agh-network/v0",
674	  "id": "msg_direct_01",
675	  "kind": "direct",
676	  "channel": "builders",
677	  "from": "patch-worker",
678	  "to": "ops-coordinator",
679	  "interaction_id": "int_patch_42",
680	  "reply_to": "msg_say_01",
681	  "trace_id": "trace_ops_patch_42",
682	  "causation_id": "msg_say_01",
683	  "ts": 1775606400,
684	  "expires_at": 1775607000,
685	  "body": {
686	    "text": "I can take the failing migration tests and send back a patch summary.",
687	    "intent": "handoff",
688	    "artifacts": []
689	  },
690	  "proof": null,
691	  "ext": {}
692	}
693	```
694	
695	### 8.6 `capability`
696	
697	`capability` transfers one full capability document. It is the explicit transfer path for the same structured capability model used by local authoring and rich discovery.
698	
699	#### Body
700	
701	```json
702	{
703	  "capability": {
704	    "id": "stable capability identifier",
705	    "summary": "short summary",
706	    "outcome": "expected result",
707	    "version": "optional semantic or content version",
708	    "digest": "sha256:...",
709	    "context_needed": [],
710	    "artifacts_expected": [],
711	    "execution_outline": [],
712	    "constraints": [],
713	    "examples": [],
714	    "requirements": []
715	  }
716	}
717	```
718	
719	#### Rules
720	
721	- `body.capability` is REQUIRED
722	- `capability.id` is REQUIRED
723	- `capability.summary` is REQUIRED
724	- `capability.outcome` is REQUIRED
725	- `capability.digest` is REQUIRED
726	- `capability.version` MAY be present
727	- `capability.requirements`, when present, MUST contain non-empty unique capability IDs after normalization
728	- senders MUST compute `capability.digest` from the canonical structured capability document
729	- receivers MUST reject digest mismatches as `verification_failed`
730	- `capability` MAY be broadcast or directed
731	- `capability` MAY open or continue an interaction when `interaction_id` is present
732	- v0 validates `requirements` syntactically; receivers do not need to resolve every referenced capability ID locally before transport
733	
734	### 8.7 `receipt`
735	
736	`receipt` acknowledges or rejects protocol-level admission and can communicate terminal cancellation.
737	
738	#### Body
739	
740	```json
741	{
742	  "for_id": "message id",
743	  "status": "accepted",
744	  "reason_code": null,
745	  "detail": null
746	}
747	```
748	
749	#### Status values
750	
751	- `accepted`
752	- `rejected`
753	- `duplicate`
754	- `expired`
755	- `unsupported`
756	- `canceled`
757	
758	### 8.8 `trace`
759	
760	`trace` reports progress or terminal outcome for an interaction.
761	
762	#### Body
763	
764	```json
765	{
766	  "state": "working",
767	  "message": "optional status text",
768	  "result": {},
769	  "artifact_refs": []
770	}
771	```
772	
773	#### State values
774	
775	- `working`
776	- `needs_input`
777	- `completed`
778	- `failed`
779	- `canceled`
780	
781	#### Rules
782	
783	- `interaction_id` is REQUIRED
784	- `trace.state` is REQUIRED
785	- terminal states SHOULD be emitted exactly once per interaction by a well-behaved sender

788	
789	## 9. Delivery and Error Model
790	
791	### 9.1 Semantic delivery guarantees
792	
793	The core defines semantic expectations, not transport mechanics.
794	
795	Implementations MUST assume:
796	
797	- messages MAY be duplicated
798	- messages MAY expire
799	- messages MAY arrive out of order
800	- delivery MAY fail silently
801	- senders and receivers MAY disagree on capability support
802	
803	### 9.2 What the core does not guarantee
804	
805	The core does not guarantee:
806	
807	- exactly-once delivery
808	- durable replay
809	- total ordering
810	- transport-level acknowledgements
811	- broker-backed persistence
812	
813	Those belong to transport or runtime layers.
814	
815	### 9.3 Receiver responsibilities
816	
817	A receiver SHOULD:
818	
819	- deduplicate by `id` within a local replay window
820	- reject expired messages
821	- treat invalid lifecycle transitions as application errors
822	- use `receipt` for acceptance, rejection, or unsupported conditions when practical
823	
824	### 9.4 Reason codes
825	
826	The core defines this initial reason-code registry:
827	
828	- `malformed`
829	- `expired`
830	- `duplicate`
831	- `unsupported_kind`
832	- `unsupported_profile`
833	- `verification_failed`
834	- `not_target`
835	- `not_found`
836	- `busy`
837	- `internal`
838	- `interaction_closed`
839	
840	Implementations MAY define namechanneld reason codes under `ext`.
841	
842	---
843	
844	## 10. AGH Network over NATS
845	
846	### 10.1 Scope
847	
848	This profile defines the normative v0 mapping of the core onto `NATS Core`. Durable replay and JetStream semantics are out of scope for this profile.
849	
850	### 10.2 Subject prefix
851	
852	The required subject prefix is:
853	
854	`agh.network.v0`
855	
856	### 10.3 Route token
857	
858	Each NATS peer MUST derive a subject-safe route token.
859	
860	The route token for a peer MUST be the first 32 lowercase hex characters of `SHA-256(peer_id UTF-8 bytes)`.
861	
862	When sending a directed message (`to != null`), the sender MUST derive the target's route token using the same algorithm applied to the `to` field value: `SHA-256(to UTF-8 bytes)[:32]`. The `to` field MUST contain the target's canonical `peer_id`, not a display name or alias.
863	
864	### 10.3.1 Maximum envelope size
865	
866	Implementations MUST support envelopes up to 1 MB (1,048,576 bytes) after JSON serialization. Senders SHOULD NOT emit envelopes exceeding this limit. For rich `whois` catalogs or `kind:"capability"` transfers that approach this threshold, senders SHOULD narrow the payload to the smallest relevant capability set instead of inventing an out-of-band artifact format.
867	
868	### 10.4 Subject mapping
869	
870	| Core intent            | NATS subject                                  |
871	| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
872	| Broadcast to a channel | `agh.network.v0.<channel>.broadcast`          |
873	| Direct to a peer       | `agh.network.v0.<channel>.peer.<route_token>` |
874	
875	```mermaid
876	sequenceDiagram
877	    participant A as Peer A
878	    participant NATS as NATS
879	    participant B as Peer B
880	    participant C as Peer C
881	
882	    Note over A,NATS: example channel = builders
883	    A->>NATS: PUB agh.network.v0.builders.broadcast
884	    NATS-->>B: deliver greet
885	    NATS-->>C: deliver greet
886	
887	    A->>NATS: PUB agh.network.v0.builders.peer.b_token
888	    NATS-->>B: deliver direct
889	
890	    B->>NATS: PUB agh.network.v0.builders.peer.a_token
891	    NATS-->>A: deliver trace
892	```
893	
894	### 10.5 Joining a channel
895	
896	A peer joins a channel by subscribing to the required NATS subjects and announcing its presence:
897	
898	1. Subscribe to `agh.network.v0.<channel>.broadcast`
899	2. Subscribe to its own direct subject `agh.network.v0.<channel>.peer.<own_route_token>`
900	3. SHOULD send a `greet` message to the broadcast subject
901	
902	A peer SHOULD send `greet` upon joining a channel. A peer SHOULD re-send `greet` after reconnecting to NATS following a connection loss.
903	
904	#### Presence through periodic greet
905	
906	Periodic `greet` re-announcement serves as an implicit heartbeat. The RECOMMENDED interval is 30 seconds. Receivers SHOULD maintain a local peer cache keyed by `peer_id` and expire entries that have not re-greeted within 2x the expected interval (RECOMMENDED: 60 seconds). A peer whose entry expires is considered offline for routing purposes. This mechanism provides presence awareness without requiring explicit departure signaling or membership state in the protocol.
907	
908	### 10.6 Sending rules
909	
910	- messages with `to = null` MUST be published to the broadcast subject
911	- messages with `to != null` MUST be published to the target peer direct subject
912	- `greet` SHOULD be broadcast
913	- targeted `whois`, `direct`, `capability`, `receipt`, and `trace` SHOULD use direct subjects
914	
915	### 10.7 Reliability posture
916	
917	This profile assumes `NATS Core` style behavior:
918	
919	- best-effort delivery
920	- no mandatory persistence
921	- no broker-managed replay
922	
923	Application-level `receipt` is therefore the normative acknowledgement mechanism at the protocol layer.
924	
925	### 10.8 Timeouts and retries
926	
927	The profile allows local retry policy, but the policy is implementation-defined.
928	
929	If a sender retries a logical message, it SHOULD preserve the same `id` so receivers can deduplicate it.
930	
931	### 10.9 Out of scope
932	
933	This v0 NATS profile does not define:
934	
935	- JetStream durability classes
936	- dead-letter semantics
937	- broker cluster topology
938	- account, tenancy, or ACL standards
939	- NATS request/reply correlation (deferred to v1)
940	
941	---
942	
943	## 11. Security Considerations
944	
945	### 11.1 Core security posture
946	
947	The core is designed around least-trust assumptions:
948	
949	- messages may be duplicated
950	- senders may be unknown
951	- transport authentication is not assumed
952	
953	### 11.2 Replay and duplication
954	
955	Implementations SHOULD maintain a bounded replay window using:
956	
957	- `id`
958	- `ts`
959	- local receipt history
960	
961	When `expires_at` is null, receivers SHOULD apply a maximum age check against `ts`. A RECOMMENDED default is to reject messages whose `ts` is more than 300 seconds in the past relative to the receiver's clock. This prevents indefinite replay of messages that carry no explicit expiration. Implementations MAY adjust this threshold but SHOULD document their chosen value.
962	
963	### 11.3 Expiration
964	
965	If `expires_at` is present and in the past, receivers SHOULD reject the message and MAY emit a `receipt` with `status = expired`.
966	
967	### 11.4 Identity in v0
968	
969	v0 does not verify sender identity. The `from` field is trusted at face value. Deployments requiring identity assurance SHOULD use transport-level security (NATS TLS, credentials) or upgrade to v1.
970	
971	### 11.5 Capability confusion
972	
973	Capability strings and rich capability metadata are advisory until a peer verifies actual behavior. Receivers MUST NOT assume advertised capabilities are safe, authorized, or guaranteed simply because they were present in a `Peer Card` or returned through capability discovery.
974	
975	---
976	
977	## 12. Upgrade to v1
978	
979	v1 adds:
980	
981	- `AGH Network Baseline Trust Profile` (Ed25519 + JCS canonicalization)
982	- `verified` and `rejected` trust states
983	- verified sender identity format (`nickname@fingerprint`)
984	- proof-stripping detection (verified-format `from` without `proof` is `rejected`)
985	- formal conformance levels (Core Sender, Core Receiver, Core Peer, NATS Peer, Verified Peer)
986	- normative extension namespacing (v0 RECOMMENDED conventions become MUST)
987	- NATS request/reply correlation
988	- fingerprint-based route token for verified peers (replaces SHA-256 derivation)
989	- new NATS subject prefix `agh.network.v1` (peers supporting both versions subscribe to both)
990	
991	The wire format is identical. The `protocol` field changes from `agh-network/v0` to `agh-network/v1`.
992

1	package globaldb
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"database/sql"
6		"errors"
7		"fmt"
8		"strings"
9	
10		"github.com/pedronauck/agh/internal/store"
11	)
12	
13	type networkMessageCursor struct {
14		MessageID string
15		Timestamp string
16	}
17	
18	// WriteNetworkMessage stores one persisted network timeline envelope, ignoring duplicate message ids.
19	func (g *GlobalDB) WriteNetworkMessage(ctx context.Context, entry store.NetworkMessageEntry) error {
20		if err := g.checkReady(ctx, "write network message"); err != nil {
21			return err
22		}
23		if err := entry.Validate(); err != nil {
24			return fmt.Errorf("store: validate network message entry: %w", err)
25		}
26		if entry.Timestamp.IsZero() {
27			entry.Timestamp = g.now()
28		}
29	
30		if _, err := g.db.ExecContext(
31			ctx,
32			`INSERT INTO network_timeline_log (
33				message_id,
34				session_id,
35				channel,
36				direction,
37				peer_from,
38				peer_to,
39				kind,
40				interaction_id,
41				reply_to,
42				trace_id,
43				causation_id,
44				intent,
45				text,
46				preview_text,
47				body_json,
48				timestamp
49			) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
50			ON CONFLICT(message_id) DO NOTHING`,
51			entry.MessageID,
52			store.NullableString(entry.SessionID),
53			entry.Channel,
54			entry.Direction,
55			entry.PeerFrom,
56			store.NullableString(entry.PeerTo),
57			entry.Kind,
58			store.NullableString(entry.InteractionID),
59			store.NullableString(entry.ReplyTo),
60			store.NullableString(entry.TraceID),
61			store.NullableString(entry.CausationID),
62			store.NullableString(entry.Intent),
63			store.NullableString(entry.Text),
64			entry.PreviewText,
65			string(entry.Body),
66			store.FormatTimestamp(entry.Timestamp),
67		); err != nil {
68			return fmt.Errorf("store: insert network message entry: %w", err)
69		}
70	
71		return nil
72	}
73	
74	// ListNetworkMessages returns persisted network timeline rows filtered by the supplied options.
75	func (g *GlobalDB) ListNetworkMessages(
76		ctx context.Context,
77		query store.NetworkMessageQuery,
78	) (entries []store.NetworkMessageEntry, err error) {
79		if err := g.checkReady(ctx, "list network messages"); err != nil {
80			return nil, err
81		}
82		if err := query.Validate(); err != nil {
83			return nil, fmt.Errorf("store: validate network message query: %w", err)
84		}
85	
86		sqlQuery, args, reverseResults, err := g.buildNetworkMessageListQuery(ctx, query)
87		if err != nil {
88			return nil, err
89		}
90	
91		rows, err := g.db.QueryContext(ctx, sqlQuery, args...)
92		if err != nil {
93			return nil, fmt.Errorf("store: query network messages: %w", err)
94		}
95		defer func() {
96			if closeErr := rows.Close(); closeErr != nil {
97				closeErr = fmt.Errorf("store: close network messages rows: %w", closeErr)
98				if err != nil {
99					err = errors.Join(err, closeErr)
100					return
101				}
102				err = closeErr
103			}
104		}()
105	
106		entries, err = loadNetworkMessageEntries(rows)
107		if err != nil {
108			return nil, err
109		}
110		if reverseResults {
111			reverseNetworkMessages(entries)
112		}
113	
114		return entries, nil
115	}
116	
117	func (g *GlobalDB) buildNetworkMessageListQuery(
118		ctx context.Context,
119		query store.NetworkMessageQuery,
120	) (string, []any, bool, error) {
121		sqlQuery := `SELECT
122			message_id,
123			session_id,
124			channel,
125			direction,
126			peer_from,
127			peer_to,
128			kind,
129			interaction_id,
130			reply_to,
131			trace_id,
132			causation_id,
133			intent,
134			text,
135			preview_text,
136			body_json,
137			timestamp
138		FROM network_timeline_log`
139	
140		where, args := networkMessageFilterClauses(query, true)
141	
142		reverseResults := false
143		switch {
144		case strings.TrimSpace(query.BeforeMessageID) != "":
145			cursor, cursorErr := g.lookupNetworkMessageCursor(ctx, query.BeforeMessageID, query)
146			if cursorErr != nil {
147				return "", nil, false, cursorErr
148			}
149			where = append(where, "(timestamp < ? OR (timestamp = ? AND message_id < ?))")
150			args = append(args, cursor.Timestamp, cursor.Timestamp, cursor.MessageID)
151			reverseResults = true
152		case strings.TrimSpace(query.AfterMessageID) != "":
153			cursor, cursorErr := g.lookupNetworkMessageCursor(ctx, query.AfterMessageID, query)
154			if cursorErr != nil {
155				return "", nil, false, cursorErr
156			}
157			where = append(where, "(timestamp > ? OR (timestamp = ? AND message_id > ?))")
158			args = append(args, cursor.Timestamp, cursor.Timestamp, cursor.MessageID)
159		}
160	
161		sqlQuery = store.AppendWhere(sqlQuery, where)
162		if reverseResults {
163			sqlQuery += " ORDER BY timestamp DESC, message_id DESC"
164		} else {
165			sqlQuery += " ORDER BY timestamp ASC, message_id ASC"
166		}
167		sqlQuery, args = store.AppendLimit(sqlQuery, args, query.Limit)
168		return sqlQuery, args, reverseResults, nil
169	}
170	
171	func networkMessageFilterClauses(query store.NetworkMessageQuery, includeMessageID bool) ([]string, []any) {
172		messageID := ""
173		if includeMessageID {
174			messageID = query.MessageID
175		}
176		where, args := store.BuildClauses(
177			store.StringClause("session_id", query.SessionID),
178			store.StringClause("channel", query.Channel),
179			store.StringClause("peer_from", query.PeerFrom),
180			store.StringClause("peer_to", query.PeerTo),
181			store.StringClause("kind", query.Kind),
182			store.StringClause("direction", query.Direction),
183			store.StringClause("message_id", messageID),
184			store.TimeClause("timestamp", ">=", query.Since),
185		)
186		if peerID := strings.TrimSpace(query.PeerID); peerID != "" {
187			where = append(where, "(peer_from = ? OR peer_to = ?)")
188			args = append(args, peerID, peerID)
189		}
190		if query.DirectedOnly {
191			where = append(where, "peer_to IS NOT NULL AND TRIM(peer_to) <> ''")
192		}
193		return where, args
194	}
195	
196	func loadNetworkMessageEntries(rows *sql.Rows) ([]store.NetworkMessageEntry, error) {
197		entries := make([]store.NetworkMessageEntry, 0)
198		for rows.Next() {
199			entry, err := scanNetworkMessage(rows)
200			if err != nil {
201				return nil, err
202			}
203			entries = append(entries, entry)
204		}
205		if err := rows.Err(); err != nil {
206			return nil, fmt.Errorf("store: iterate network messages: %w", err)
207		}
208		return entries, nil
209	}
210	
211	func reverseNetworkMessages(entries []store.NetworkMessageEntry) {
212		for left, right := 0, len(entries)-1; left < right; left, right = left+1, right-1 {
213			entries[left], entries[right] = entries[right], entries[left]
214		}
215	}
216	
217	func (g *GlobalDB) lookupNetworkMessageCursor(
218		ctx context.Context,
219		messageID string,
220		query store.NetworkMessageQuery,
221	) (networkMessageCursor, error) {
222		trimmed := strings.TrimSpace(messageID)
223		if trimmed == "" {
224			return networkMessageCursor{}, nil
225		}
226	
227		where, args := networkMessageFilterClauses(query, false)
228		where = append([]string{"message_id = ?"}, where...)
229		args = append([]any{trimmed}, args...)
230	
231		var cursor networkMessageCursor
232		err := g.db.QueryRowContext(
233			ctx,
234			store.AppendWhere(`SELECT message_id, timestamp FROM network_timeline_log`, where),
235			args...,
236		).Scan(&cursor.MessageID, &cursor.Timestamp)
237		if err != nil {
238			if errors.Is(err, sql.ErrNoRows) {
239				return networkMessageCursor{}, fmt.Errorf("store: network message cursor not found: %w", err)
240			}
241			return networkMessageCursor{}, fmt.Errorf("store: lookup network message cursor: %w", err)
242		}
243		return cursor, nil
244	}
245	
246	func scanNetworkMessage(scanner rowScanner) (store.NetworkMessageEntry, error) {
247		var (
248			entry         store.NetworkMessageEntry
249			sessionID     sql.NullString
250			peerTo        sql.NullString
251			interactionID sql.NullString
252			replyTo       sql.NullString
253			traceID       sql.NullString
254			causationID   sql.NullString
255			intent        sql.NullString
256			text          sql.NullString
257			previewText   string
258			bodyRaw       string
259			timestampRaw  string
260		)
261		if err := scanner.Scan(
262			&entry.MessageID,
263			&sessionID,
264			&entry.Channel,
265			&entry.Direction,
266			&entry.PeerFrom,
267			&peerTo,
268			&entry.Kind,
269			&interactionID,
270			&replyTo,
271			&traceID,
272			&causationID,
273			&intent,
274			&text,
275			&previewText,
276			&bodyRaw,
277			&timestampRaw,
278		); err != nil {
279			return store.NetworkMessageEntry{}, fmt.Errorf("store: scan network message: %w", err)
280		}
281	
282		if value := store.NullString(sessionID); value != nil {
283			entry.SessionID = *value
284		}
285		if value := store.NullString(peerTo); value != nil {
286			entry.PeerTo = *value
287		}
288		if value := store.NullString(interactionID); value != nil {
289			entry.InteractionID = *value
290		}
291		if value := store.NullString(replyTo); value != nil {
292			entry.ReplyTo = *value
293		}
294		if value := store.NullString(traceID); value != nil {
295			entry.TraceID = *value
296		}
297		if value := store.NullString(causationID); value != nil {
298			entry.CausationID = *value
299		}
300		if value := store.NullString(intent); value != nil {
301			entry.Intent = *value
302		}
303		if value := store.NullString(text); value != nil {
304			entry.Text = *value
305		}
306		entry.PreviewText = strings.TrimSpace(previewText)
307		entry.Body = []byte(bodyRaw)
308	
309		timestamp, err := store.ParseTimestamp(timestampRaw)
310		if err != nil {
311			return store.NetworkMessageEntry{}, fmt.Errorf("store: parse network message timestamp: %w", err)
312		}
313		entry.Timestamp = timestamp
314		return entry, nil
315	}
316
