1	# Standing Directives
2	
3	Ongoing engineering posture, not date-stamped per-task plans. These are perpetually active rules. Surfaced from undated entries in `.codex/plans/` and verified across the synthesis corpus.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## SD-001 — Long-Running Sessions Supervision
8	
9	**Posture.** AGH sessions can run for hours. Supervise activity, don't wait on wall-clock timeouts. Heartbeats, progress events, and idempotent cancel are the supervision primitives.
10	
11	**Required behavior:**
12	
13	- Activity supervisor per prompt/session inspired by Hermes; no wall-clock timeouts as the supervision primitive.
14	- Heartbeats update **metadata only** — never flow through the ACP event channel (backpressure risk). `runtime_progress` is a low-cadence persisted event.
15	- Warnings emit exactly once per session per cause; subsequent inactivity triggers cancel-with-grace before `StopTimeout`.
16	- `CancelPrompt`, session stop, and timeout collapse into ONE idempotent cancellation path.
17	- Inactivity timeout MUST NOT be implemented as a wall-clock timeout. Heartbeat must be positive; `0` disables warning/timeout/progress.
18	- Configuration under `[session.supervision]` with explicit zero-value semantics.
19	
20	**Source:** `.codex/plans/long-running-sessions.md` (undated standing directive). Hermes Track 03 ACP & Session Lifecycle Hardening implements this.
21	
22	**Triggers re-evaluation when:** any change to `internal/session/manager_*.go`, `internal/acp/client.go` lifecycle paths, or supervisor configuration is proposed.
23	
24	---
25	
26	## SD-002 — Remove Legacy Alpha Compatibility Code
27	
28	**Posture.** AGH is greenfield alpha with zero production users. Compat shims, nil-receiver legacy stubs, legacy-meta no-ops, and dual code paths for "old behavior" are forbidden. **Delete the old thing.** This is a stronger, perpetual application of the CLAUDE.md "Greenfield Alpha — Zero Legacy Tolerance" rule.
29	
30	**Required behavior:**
31	
32	- Strip nil-receiver fallbacks, `legacySessionMeta` mappers, no-op compat methods, and "preserve old field name" branches.
33	- Renames are **hard cuts**: code, storage, APIs, CLI, extensions, specs, RFCs, and `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts in the same change. No aliases, no dual fields.
34	- Distinguish "still useful current logic" (`Reconcile` at composition root) from "legacy support" (delete). Composition-root reconciliation is NOT legacy.
35	- One-pass legacy repair is allowed only in the narrow case where the cost of "delete the old thing" is "every developer rebuilds their local SQLite". Document the boundary in an ADR (e.g., `session-driver-override/adrs/adr-005.md`) and switch to strict semantics immediately after repair.
36	
37	**Source:** `.codex/plans/remove-legacy-alpha.md` (undated standing directive). Multiple ADRs reinforce.
38	
39	**Triggers re-evaluation when:** any PR proposing schema-version branching, `// legacy` markers, dual-naming, or "preserve for compat" logic.
40	
41	---
42	
43	## SD-003 — Conversation in BR-PT, Artifacts in English
44	
45	**Posture.** Pedro types/speaks in Brazilian Portuguese; all persistent artifacts are English.
46	
47	**Required behavior:**
48	
49	- Respond in BR-PT when prompted in BR-PT.
50	- TechSpecs, ADRs, `_idea.md`, `_tasks.md`, code, tests, comments, commit messages, documentation, ledger files, memory files: English.
51	- Verbatim user quotes preserved in evidence/research artifacts may keep BR-PT (because they're evidence).
52	- BR-PT pushback markers ("fraco", "leviano", "ruim", "está totalmente errado", "meia boca", "esquecendo coisas") are escalation signals — slow down and re-clarify.
53	
54	**Source:** `.codex/ledger/` notes; recurring pattern across all sessions.
55	
56	---
57	
58	## SD-004 — Multi-LLM Development Pipeline
59	
60	**Posture.** AGH development uses three LLMs with deliberate role assignment.
61	
62	**Required behavior:**
63	
64	- **Codex (`gpt-5.4` with `reasoning_effort=xhigh`)** authors TechSpecs, major Go code, autonomous-mode kernel work.
65	- **Claude Opus (`xhigh`)** pressure-tests TechSpecs in user-directed cross-LLM review rounds, reviews architecture decisions, writes/reviews React/E2E frontend code.
66	- **`gpt-5.4-mini` with `reasoning_effort=high`** runs as parallel subagents for breadth (codebase mapping, competitor analysis, conversation-log auditing) when explicitly delegated.
67	- Do not substitute models without explicit user approval.
68	- Subagents are read-only — they return analysis to the parent agent, and the parent writes any required files.
69	
70	**Source:** Direct quotes across many sessions; codified in `feedback_multi_llm_pipeline.md` (user memory).
71	
72	---
73	
74	## SD-005 — Real-Scenario QA Before Release
75	
76	**Posture.** `make verify` is necessary but not sufficient. Real-scenario QA against a multi-agent / multi-channel / multi-task workspace catches drift `make verify` misses.
77	
78	**Required behavior:**
79	
80	- Every program ends with a `qa-report` task and a `qa-execution` task. UI-bearing features include browser-based e2e.
81	- Use the `real-scenario-qa` skill for release validation; it delegates to `qa-execution` and `qa-report` for inner mechanics.
82	- Hermetic QA still respects each provider's auth contract: bound-secret and brokered lanes isolate `PROVIDER_HOME`, while `native_cli` providers with `home_policy=operator` keep the operator `HOME` / native login state unless the scenario explicitly validates isolated provider-home behavior.
83	- Concrete bug evidence (autonomy task_18 BUG-001/002/003, Hermes BUG-001..007) shows the QA pass surfaces real production bugs the unit/lint/build coverage cannot catch.
84	
85	**Source:** Codex sessions (most-repeated request); `real-scenario-qa` SKILL.md; autonomy and Hermes QA verification reports.
86	
87	---
88	
89	## SD-006 — Forensic-First Bug Fixes
90	
91	**Posture.** Every bug-fix plan opens with a confirmed reproduction (timestamp, command, observed evidence) BEFORE listing changes. "I think" or "probably" at the top of a fix plan is forbidden.
92	
93	**Required behavior:**
94	
95	- Reproduce the bug with the narrowest real command before editing code.
96	- Record reproduction in the plan: timestamp, exact command, observed output.
97	- Distinguish symptom from root cause in writing.
98	- Fix at root cause; don't patch symptoms.
99	- Add focused regression coverage at the correct layer.
100	- Re-run the narrow reproduction, the impacted scenario, and relevant package tests.
101	
102	**Source:** `.codex/plans/` (consistent forensic frame in `child-workgroup-activation.md`, `session-stop-hang.md`, `dashboard-xterm-visibility.md`, `prompt-stream-stall.md`); also encoded in `real-scenario-qa` and `cy-fix-reviews`.
103	
104	---
105	
106	## SD-007 — Truthful UI > Plausible UI
107	
108	**Posture.** UI must reflect actual backend support. Don't render controls or metrics the runtime doesn't model.
109	
110	**Required behavior:**
111	
112	- When Paper artboards (design references) conflict with daemon truth, **daemon wins**.
113	- Paper governs _composition_; `DESIGN.md` governs _grammar_ (tokens, depth, motion).
114	- No invented controls (per-bridge retry/timeout when runtime doesn't support them).
115	- No invented metrics (no "pending retry" counts when telemetry doesn't expose them).
116	- Observability-only views are allowed (e.g., Network Peers in v1 has no Disconnect/Remove until backend models them).
117	
118	**Source:** Multiple plans in `.codex/plans/` (automation-bridges-paper-redesign, network-paper-pages, bridge-web-e2e).
119	
120	---
121	
122	## SD-008 — Composition Root Discipline
123	
124	**Posture.** Only `daemon/` wires components. Reconciliation logic running at boot belongs to composition root and is NOT "legacy support."
125	
126	**Required behavior:**
127	
128	- New cross-cutting wiring goes in `internal/daemon/`. Never in subordinate packages.
129	- Boot reconciliation (`Reconcile`) is composition-root current logic, not legacy.
130	- Subordinate packages define interfaces and accept implementations via constructors / functional options.
131	- No back-pointers between subordinate packages.
132	- The package import graph flows downward only. `internal/daemon` is the only multi-importer.
133	- `mage Boundaries` is the CI-enforced check; update it in the same commit that introduces a new internal subpackage.
134	
135	**Source:** Root CLAUDE.md Architecture Principles; `_techspec.md` autonomy boundaries; `.codex/plans/observability-spine.md`, `kb-refac-full-sweep.md`, `remove-legacy-alpha.md`.
136	
137	---
138	
139	## SD-009 — Data Exists / Consumer Missing — Build the Consumer
140	
141	**Posture.** When multiple independent investigations converge on the same data structure as "right shape but unconsumed", the gap is integration ergonomics, not architecture.
142	
143	**Required behavior:**
144	
145	- Don't redesign the data when independent slices flag the same data as "right shape, no consumer."
146	- Build the consumer; preserve the data shape.
147	- Surface convergence explicitly in research artifacts ("8 of 10 slices flagged the same six lines").
148	- The autonomy program is the canonical case study: AGH was 80% built before autonomy started; the work was integration, not invention.
149	
150	**Source:** `analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` finding 7; `autonomous/analysis/analysis.md`.
151	
152	---
153	
154	## SD-010 — Detached Execution Lifetime
155	
156	**Posture.** Any work that outlives an HTTP/UDS request — prompts, network channel sends, automation jobs — MUST detach via `context.WithoutCancel(ctx)`. Never tie execution lifetime to request lifetime.
157	
158	**Required behavior:**
159	
160	- Call long-lived work with `context.WithoutCancel(c.Request.Context())` so client disconnect stops streaming, not execution.
161	- Expose explicit cancel endpoints (e.g., `POST /api/sessions/:id/prompt/cancel`).
162	- `context.WithoutCancel` does NOT preserve deadlines — re-attach a deadline if needed.
163	- The four-cause prompt-stream-stall incident (2026-04-20) is the canonical illustration: HTTP request lifetime tied to prompt → tool_call closed stream → web stop using transport abort → metadata repair classifying `m.pending` as crashed. Each was a separate symptom of the same lifetime-coupling root cause.
164	
165	**Source:** `.codex/plans/prompt-stream-stall.md`; `_synthesis.md` lesson L1 (4-analysis evidence).
166	
167	---
168	
169	## SD-011 — Extensible and Agent-Manageable by Design
170	
171	**Posture.** AGH is not only a daemon with UI. It is an extensible runtime that agents must be able to inspect, configure, operate, and repair through structured surfaces. A feature is incomplete if it cannot be extended by AGH's extension surfaces or managed by agents without relying on the web UI.
172	
173	**Required behavior:**
174	
175	- Every PRD, TechSpec, `_tasks.md`, and task body that creates, updates, or removes a feature states the impact on AGH extensibility surfaces: extensions, hooks, skills/capabilities, tools/resources, bundles, registries, bridge SDKs, MCP sidecars, and protocol docs.
176	- Every user-visible or operator-visible capability has an agent-manageability plan: CLI verbs with structured output, HTTP/UDS parity when daemon state crosses the boundary, deterministic error contracts, discoverable status, and documentation for the agent path.
177	- Every CLI command, HTTP endpoint, UDS route, generated contract type, and site reference is added, updated, or deleted in the same change as the feature it manages.
178	- Every `config.toml` addition, update, removal, or no-longer-needed key is handled as a lifecycle change: structs, defaults, merge/overlay behavior, validation, examples, docs, and tests move together.
179	- "No impact" is allowed only with evidence: the artifact names the checked surfaces and explains why no extension, agent-operation, or config change is needed.
180	
181	**Source:** explicit user directive on 2026-04-26; reinforces AGH's product premise (`agent-first`, highly extensible, highly configurable).
182	
183	**Triggers re-evaluation when:** any spec/feature changes runtime behavior, public contracts, CLI verbs, HTTP/UDS routes, config keys, hooks, extension manifests, skill/tool/resource surfaces, bridge SDKs, or agent-operated workflows.
184	
```

```
1	# TechSpec: AGH Network v0 Implementation
2	
3	## Executive Summary
4	
5	This TechSpec defines the implementation of AGH Network v0 — the agent-to-agent communication layer for the AGH daemon. The implementation adds a new `internal/network/` package that embeds a NATS server in the daemon, manages peer lifecycle as sessions join/leave spaces, routes messages between agents, and exposes network operations via CLI commands and a bundled skill.
6	
7	**Key architectural decisions:**
8	
9	- Embedded NATS server in the daemon binary (single-binary, local-first)
10	- Each active session is a unique peer with identity `{agent_name}.{session_id}`
11	- Network Manager as boot-phase observer of session lifecycle (not a property of sessions)
12	- Outbound: agents send messages via `agh network` CLI commands through terminal tools
13	- Inbound: daemon auto-prompts sessions with queued network messages after current turn completes
14	- Runtime-created spaces with explicit session opt-in via `--space` flag (no space catalog in config)
15	- Peer state in-memory and reconstructable; audit log persisted to globaldb + flat file
16	- v0 network runtime remains a transport/router/correlation layer, not a workflow engine
17	
18	**Primary trade-off:** CLI-based outbound messaging has ~50ms subprocess overhead per command, but reuses existing infrastructure (terminal tools, UDS transport, skills system) and avoids new MCP server implementation. MCP tools can be added to the skill later without breaking changes.
19	
20	**NATS boundary exception:** CLAUDE.md states "no NATS" and "direct function calls through interfaces." The embedded NATS server is a transport-boundary exception for the AGH Network profile (RFC v0 Section 10). It does not authorize internal package communication through a generic bus — NATS is used exclusively within `internal/network/` for the wire protocol, not as an inter-package event system.
21	
22	---
23	
24	## System Architecture
25	
26	### Component Overview
27	
28	```
29	                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
30	                    │              AGH Daemon                  │
31	                    │                                         │
32	                    │  ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐  │
33	                    │  │ Session  │────▶│ Network Manager  │  │
34	                    │  │ Manager  │     │                  │  │
35	                    │  └──────────┘     │  ┌────────────┐  │  │
36	                    │       │           │  │ Peer       │  │  │
37	                    │       │           │  │ Registry   │  │  │
38	                    │       ▼           │  └────────────┘  │  │
39	                    │  ┌──────────┐     │  ┌────────────┐  │  │
40	                    │  │ Session  │◀───▶│  │ Message    │  │  │
41	                    │  │ (Peer A) │     │  │ Router     │  │  │
42	                    │  └──────────┘     │  └────────────┘  │  │
43	                    │  ┌──────────┐     │  ┌────────────┐  │  │
44	                    │  │ Session  │◀───▶│  │ Embedded   │  │  │
45	                    │  │ (Peer B) │     │  │ NATS Server│  │  │
46	                    │  └──────────┘     │  └────────────┘  │  │
47	                    │                   └──────────────────┘  │
48	                    │                                         │
49	                    │  ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐  │
50	                    │  │   CLI    │────▶│   UDS Server     │  │
51	                    │  └──────────┘     └──────────────────┘  │
52	                    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
53	
54	  Outbound: Agent → terminal tool → `agh network send` → CLI → UDS → Network Manager → NATS
55	  Inbound:  NATS → Network Manager → queue → session/prompt → Agent
56	```
57	
58	**Components:**
59	
60	| Component       | Responsibility                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
61	| --------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
62	| Network Manager | Boot-phase lifecycle component. Owns embedded NATS server, peer registry, message routing, inbound delivery.                                                                                                                                                                                  |
63	| Peer Registry   | Two-tier in-memory registry: (1) local peers — maps session IDs to peer identity and space memberships for sessions on this daemon; (2) remote peer cache — maps peer_id to Peer Card and last-seen timestamp, populated from received `greet` messages, expired after 2x heartbeat interval. |
64	| Message Router  | Receives NATS messages, validates envelopes, routes to target sessions.                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
65	| Embedded NATS   | `nats-server/v2` embedded in daemon. In-process connection for daemon; loopback TCP listener is transport-internal and not a supported client interface for AGH local clients in v0.                                                                                                          |
66	| CLI Commands    | `agh network {send,peers,spaces,status,inbox}` — outbound path for agents and humans.                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
67	| Bundled Skill   | `agh-network` skill with SKILL.md instructions for agents on how to use the network.                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
68	| Inbound Queue   | Per-session message buffer. Delivers via `session/prompt` when agent is idle.                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
69	
70	### Data Flow: Complete Message Lifecycle
71	
72	**Outbound (Agent A sends `direct` to Agent B):**
73	
74	1. Agent A calls terminal tool:
75	   ```
76	   agh network send --session sess-abc \
77	     --space builders --kind direct --to reviewer.sess-xyz \
78	     --interaction-id int_patch_42 --reply-to msg_say_01 \
79	     --trace-id trace_ops_42 --causation-id msg_say_01 \
80	     --body '{"text":"Please inspect auth.go and tell me what is failing.","intent":"review_request"}'
81	   ```
82	2. CLI parses args, connects to daemon via UDS
83	3. UDS handler calls `NetworkManager.Send(ctx, SendRequest{...})`
84	4. Network Manager constructs AGH envelope:
85	   - Uses caller-provided `--id` if present (for retries with preserved ID), otherwise generates collision-resistant ID
86	   - Sets `protocol: "agh-network/v0"`, `kind: "direct"`, `space: "builders"`
87	   - Treats `--session` as a daemon-local session selector, NOT as a caller-supplied peer identity claim
88	   - Validates the selected session is active on this daemon and derives `from` from daemon-owned session metadata → `"coder.sess-abc"`
89	   - Sets `to`, `interaction_id`, `reply_to`, `trace_id`, `causation_id`, `expires_at` from CLI flags
90	   - Sets `ts: now()`
91	   - Validates envelope against RFC schema including per-kind required fields (RFC v0 Section 5.1.2):
92	     - `direct`: `to` MUST be present, `interaction_id` MUST be present
93	     - `receipt`: `interaction_id` MUST be present; `to` MUST be present for targeted (directed) receipts
94	     - `trace`: `interaction_id` MUST be present; `to` MUST be present for targeted (directed) traces
95	     - targeted `whois`: `to` SHOULD be present; `whois` response: `reply_to` MUST be present
96	     - `greet`: `to` SHOULD be null, `interaction_id` SHOULD be null
97	     - `say`: `to` SHOULD be null
98	   - Validates `space` matches `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,63}`
99	   - Validates `from` and `to` match `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9._-]{0,127}`
100	5. For directed sends, performs sender-side presence preflight against the local session registry and remote peer cache:
101	   - If `to` is missing from the current presence view, returns `not_found` locally and DOES NOT publish
102	   - If `to` is present but expired in the remote cache, returns `not_found` locally and DOES NOT publish
103	6. Derives route token: `SHA-256("reviewer.sess-xyz")[:32]`
104	7. Publishes to NATS subject: `agh.network.v0.builders.peer.<route_token>`
105	8. Returns envelope `id` to CLI → agent receives success as JSON
106	
107	**Inbound (Agent B receives the `direct`):**
108	
109	1. NATS delivers message to Network Manager's subscription on `agh.network.v0.builders.peer.<own_route_token>`
110	2. Message Router validates:
111	   - Required fields present
112	   - Well-formed envelope
113	   - Not expired (`expires_at` check)
114	   - Deduplication by `id` within replay window
115	3. Router resolves target session from peer registry
116	4. Checks session state:
117	   - **Idle**: calls `SessionPrompter.PromptNetwork(ctx, sessionID, formattedMessage)` immediately
118	   - **Busy** (active turn): enqueues message in per-session inbound queue
119	5. When agent's current turn completes, queue drains: next message delivered via `PromptNetwork()`
120	6. Agent receives formatted prompt with structural delimiter:
121	
122	   ```xml
123	   <network-message id="msg_direct_01" from="coder.sess-abc" space="builders"
124	     kind="direct" interaction="int_patch_42" trust="untrusted">
125	     <network-preview encoding="xml-escaped">Please inspect auth.go and tell me what is failing.</network-preview>
126	     <network-body encoding="base64-json">eyJ0ZXh0IjoiUGxlYXNlIGluc3BlY3QgYXV0aC5nbyBhbmQgdGVsbCBtZSB3aGF0IGlzIGZhaWxpbmcuIiwiaW50ZW50IjoicmV2aWV3X3JlcXVlc3QifQ==</network-body>
127	   </network-message>
128	
129	   Use `agh network send` to respond. See `agh network --help` for options.
130	   ```
131	
132	7. Agent processes and may send response via CLI tool
133	8. Daemon records sent/received messages to audit log
134	
135	---
136	
137	## Implementation Design
138	
139	### Core Interfaces
140	
141	```go
142	// Manager is the top-level network orchestrator.
143	// Initialized at daemon boot, observes session lifecycle.
144	type Manager struct {
145	    mu           sync.RWMutex
146	    config       Config
147	    logger       *slog.Logger
148	    natsServer   *server.Server
149	    natsConn     *nats.Conn
150	    natsToken    string
151	    peers        *PeerRegistry
152	    router       *Router
153	    queues       map[string]*InboundQueue // sessionID -> queue
154	    sessions     SessionSource
155	    prompter     SessionPrompter
156	    auditor      AuditWriter
157	    lifecycleCtx context.Context
158	    deliveries   sync.Map // sessionID -> *deliveryState (one active worker per session)
159	}
160	
161	// SessionSource provides read access to active sessions.
162	type SessionSource interface {
163	    Get(id string) (SessionInfo, bool)
164	    List() []SessionInfo
165	}
166	
167	// SessionPrompter delivers network messages to sessions.
168	// Implemented by a daemon adapter that wraps session.Manager.PromptWithOpts()
169	// with network-specific behavior:
170	//   - Tags the turn as network-originated (TurnSource metadata)
171	//   - Drains the returned <-chan acp.AgentEvent internally
172	//   - Propagates TurnSource through ACP handler chain for tool blocking
173	//
174	// This is safe because no session locks are held during
175	// pumpPrompt() or dispatchTurnEnd() (verified by mutex analysis).
176	type SessionPrompter interface {
177	    PromptNetwork(ctx context.Context, sessionID string, message string) error
178	    IsPrompting(sessionID string) bool
179	}
180	
181	// AuditWriter records network messages for post-incident investigation.
182	type AuditWriter interface {
183	    RecordSent(ctx context.Context, envelope Envelope) error
184	    RecordReceived(ctx context.Context, envelope Envelope) error
185	    RecordRejected(ctx context.Context, envelope Envelope, reason string) error
186	}
187	```
188	
189	**Interface Split (ADR-002 pattern: narrow interfaces consumed where needed):**
190	
191	```go
192	// NetworkPeerLifecycle is the narrow surface consumed by session manager.
193	// Defined in internal/network/, consumed by internal/session/.
194	type NetworkPeerLifecycle interface {
195	    JoinSpace(ctx context.Context, sessionID, peerID, space string) error
196	    LeaveSpace(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) error
197	}
198	
199	// NetworkService is the full surface consumed by CLI/API handlers.
200	// Defined in internal/api/core/, consumed by internal/api/udsapi/.
```

```
1	---
2	title: Agent Handoff and Context Transfer
3	type: wiki
4	stage: compiled
5	domain: agent-networks
6	tags:
7	  - agent-networks

19	  - "[[langgraph-multi-agent-workflows]]"
20	  - "[[langchain-benchmarking-multi-agent-architectures]]"
21	  - "[[multi-agent-memory-computer-architecture]]"
22	---
23	
24	# Agent Handoff and Context Transfer
25	
26	A handoff is the primitive that transfers control — and with it, state — from one agent to another. It is the multi-agent-system equivalent of a function tail-call: the caller stops, the callee starts, and the callee inherits enough context to continue the work. Handoffs underpin every orchestration pattern more sophisticated than "one agent, many tools." They are also the main site of degradation in multi-agent systems: every handoff is an opportunity to drop state, paraphrase away detail, or land the next agent in a posture it cannot recover from. This article works through the mechanics of the handoff primitive as implemented in the OpenAI Agents SDK, LangGraph Swarm, and LangChain's four-pattern framework — what gets transferred, what gets lost, how to type the payload, and how to test correctness.
27	
28	## Handoff as Control Transfer
29	
30	A handoff has three atomic components:
31	
32	1. **Control transfer** — the active agent stops; a new agent becomes active
33	2. **Context transfer** — the new agent receives enough state to continue
34	3. **Response attribution** — it becomes clear to the outside world (user, orchestrator) which agent is speaking
35	
36	In the OpenAI Agents SDK, a handoff is explicitly modeled as "a tool call that returns another Agent." The runner recognizes the returned Agent, switches `active_agent`, keeps the shared conversation history, and continues the loop. The agent-instructions field (the system prompt) is replaced with the new agent's instructions; the chat history persists unchanged.
37	
38	The crucial property: **only the active agent's instructions are in the system prompt at any time**, but the conversation history — user messages, prior assistant turns, tool results — is shared. This is the "phone transfer" model: the new agent has complete knowledge of what came before, but operates under its own instructions.
39	
40	A minimal handoff flow:
41	
42	```
43	User → [Agent A, system: "you are a triage agent"]
44	         → Agent A calls tool: handoff_to_refund_agent()
45	         → Runner swaps active_agent to Refund Agent
46	         → [Refund Agent, system: "you are a refund specialist"]
47	         → Refund Agent reads full history, responds
48	```
49	
50	The switch is cheap (one extra LLM turn to invoke the handoff tool) but architecturally load-bearing. Everything downstream — memory consistency, observability, testing — depends on whether the handoff preserves enough state.
51	
52	## Handoff vs Delegation vs Invocation
53	
54	Three related primitives are often conflated:
55	
56	| Primitive | Control | Shared history? | Return value |
57	|---|---|---|---|
58	| **Handoff** | Transfers (caller done) | Yes, full | None — callee continues |
59	| **Delegation** | Lent (caller awaits) | Partial (briefed) | Callee's output |
60	| **Tool invocation** | Kept (caller in charge) | No | Tool result |
61	
62	**Delegation** is the supervisor-worker pattern from [[langchain-choosing-multi-agent-architecture|LangChain's Subagents pattern]]. The supervisor passes a scoped task to a subagent with a briefing, waits for the subagent to return, and synthesizes the result. The supervisor remains in control of the user conversation. Context is *briefed*, not *shared*: the subagent only sees what the supervisor chose to pack. [[claude-code/wiki/concepts/Agent Swarm and Subagents|Claude Code's SendMessageTool mailbox]] is a production example — the Leader ships structured messages into a teammate's mailbox directory, the teammate receives only the briefing, and forked subagents go one step further by sharing the parent's full prompt prefix byte-for-byte to maximize cache hits.
63	
64	For additional delegation implementations, see [[goclaw/wiki/concepts/Agent Teams and Delegation|GoClaw's Agent Teams]] (permission-gated delegation with team task boards) and [[openfang/wiki/concepts/Autonomous Agent Runtime|OpenFang's Autonomous Agent Runtime]] (WASM sandbox for isolation with 53 builtin tools).
65	
66	**Tool invocation** is the narrowest: a deterministic function is called, a result is returned, and the calling agent keeps going.
67	
68	**Handoff** sits between them — full-context transfer with irreversible control transfer. The active agent is replaced; there is no "return." LangGraph's supervisor pattern mixes handoff and delegation: the supervisor `handoff`s to a worker, but the worker eventually handoffs back to the supervisor to continue the user conversation.
69	
70	Choosing correctly matters. [[langchain-choosing-multi-agent-architecture|LangChain's decision table]] maps patterns to structures:
71	
72	- Multiple distinct domains with parallel execution → **Subagents** (delegation)
73	- Sequential workflow with state transitions → **Handoffs**
74	- Multiple knowledge sources in parallel → **Router** (delegation)
75	- Single agent with many specializations → **Skills** (progressive disclosure)
76	
77	Handoffs specifically fit sequential workflows where the *identity* of the speaker changes — customer support routing, multi-stage form collection, specialist escalation.
78	
79	## Context Packing Strategies
80	
81	When context must be *packed* (delegation) rather than *shared* (OpenAI-style handoff), the packer chooses what survives the boundary. Four strategies dominate:
82	
83	### 1. Full history pass-through
84	
85	The callee receives every message in the conversation. Simplest, highest fidelity, most expensive. Token counts grow linearly with conversation depth. OpenAI Agents SDK handoffs do this by default.
86	
87	### 2. Summary
88	
89	The caller distills the conversation into a compact summary, then hands over the summary plus the latest user turn. Fast and cheap, but susceptible to the "telephone game" — detail, nuance, and verbatim phrasing are lost. The [[langchain-benchmarking-multi-agent-architectures|LangChain benchmarking study]] traced ~50% of supervisor-architecture errors to exactly this lossy paraphrasing.
90	
91	### 3. Snapshot (structured state)
92	
93	The caller writes structured state — user intent, collected fields, current step — and the callee reads that state plus recent messages. Less lossy than summary (state is typed), less expensive than full history. LangGraph's [[langgraph-multi-agent-workflows|state graph]] works this way: state is a typed object that flows through nodes.
94	
95	### 4. Schema-typed payload
96	
97	The handoff carries a declared schema — e.g., `CustomerIssue { order_id, complaint_type, resolved: bool }`. The callee consumes a typed record, not raw text. This is the most robust and is recommended for cross-team or cross-vendor agents; see [[Agent Capability Negotiation and Binding]] for how schemas bind to capabilities.
98	
99	Production systems often combine strategies: a schema-typed handoff payload **plus** the last N messages **plus** a rolling summary. The schema anchors critical facts; the messages preserve nuance; the summary provides cheap orientation.
100	
101	A fourth hybrid emerges in practice: **pointer-based packing**. Instead of embedding the context into the handoff message, the caller writes the context to a shared store and passes a pointer. The callee fetches on demand — only the slices it needs, when it needs them. This works well for large artifacts (uploaded PDFs, multi-page retrieval results) where embedding the full text would blow the token budget. It also interacts cleanly with [[Multi-Agent Memory Consistency]]: the pointer carries a version stamp, so the callee fetches exactly the snapshot the caller intended, not a later drift. The trade-off is an extra round trip — the callee must fetch before reasoning — but for artifacts over a few thousand tokens, the latency cost beats the token cost.
102	
103	## OpenAI Agents SDK Handoff Pattern
104	
105	The OpenAI Agents SDK formalized the handoff primitive around three design choices:
106	
107	1. **Handoffs are tools.** `handoff_to(target_agent)` is a normal tool call; the runner interprets the return value.
108	2. **History is shared.** The conversation array carries across the transfer — no re-packing required.
109	3. **System prompt is swapped.** The new agent's instructions become the first message; the old instructions vanish.
110	
111	Pattern code:
112	
113	```python
114	from agents import Agent, handoff, Runner
115	
116	refund_agent = Agent(
117	    name="Refund Specialist",
118	    instructions="Process refund requests. Verify order, issue refund, confirm to user.",
119	    tools=[verify_order, issue_refund],
120	)
121	
122	triage_agent = Agent(
123	    name="Triage",
124	    instructions="Classify user requests. Hand off to the right specialist.",
125	    handoffs=[handoff(refund_agent), handoff(support_agent)],
126	)
127	
128	result = Runner.run(triage_agent, user_input="I want a refund for order 1234")
129	```
130	
131	Under the hood, the `handoffs=[...]` list is compiled into tools on the triage agent. When triage calls `handoff_to_refund_specialist`, the runner switches `active_agent`, updates the system prompt, and continues. The refund agent sees the full prior conversation, including the original user message and triage's reasoning. It does not see triage's system prompt.
132	
133	Safety patterns layered on top:
134	
135	- **Approval gates** — wrap write tools behind a narrow "action agent" and require `execute_tools=False` + human approval for state-changing operations
136	- **Argument validation** — server-side Pydantic schemas reject malformed tool inputs
137	- **Tool allowlists** — each agent exposes only its own tools, not the caller's
138	- **Max turn caps** — hard-limit runaway handoff loops
139	
140	The SDK also ships **tracing** (integrated OpenAI evaluation/fine-tuning pipeline) and **sessions** (persistent memory across agent interactions) — both critical for [[Agent Observability and Distributed Tracing|observing]] handoffs in production.
141	
142	## LangGraph Swarm Handoffs
143	
144	LangGraph's Swarm package (released 2025) implements decentralized handoffs — no central supervisor. Each agent knows about its peers and can directly hand off via `create_handoff_tool`:
145	
146	```python
147	from langgraph_swarm import create_handoff_tool
148	
149	handoff_to_refund = create_handoff_tool(
150	    agent_name="refund_agent",
151	    description="Transfer to refund specialist when user wants a refund",
152	)
153	
154	triage_agent = create_react_agent(
155	    model=llm,
156	    tools=[handoff_to_refund, handoff_to_support, classify_intent],
157	    prompt="Triage user requests.",
158	)
159	```
160	
161	The swarm runtime maintains shared state — conversation messages, `active_agent` — and routes each turn to whichever agent is active. An agent can hand off to any peer it knows about.
162	
163	Swarm trade-offs per [[langgraph-multi-agent-workflows|LangGraph documentation]]:
164	
165	- **No bottleneck** — no single supervisor throttles throughput
166	- **Peer awareness required** — every sub-agent must know all others, which does not scale to third-party or open-ecosystem agents
167	- **Debugging challenge** — "why did the user end up at Agent F instead of Agent D?" requires [[Agent Observability and Distributed Tracing|distributed tracing]]; the 2026 warning is explicit: *if you don't have production-grade observability, don't use swarm*
168	
169	In the [[langchain-benchmarking-multi-agent-architectures|LangChain benchmark]], swarm slightly outperformed supervisor because it avoided translation overhead — agents respond to users directly rather than routing through a supervisor. But the handoff graph must be small and well-understood.
170	
171	## Typed Handoff Schemas
172	
173	When handoffs cross service boundaries or vendor lines (e.g., via [[The A2A Protocol|A2A]]), typed payloads become mandatory. A typed handoff declares:
174	
175	```python
176	from pydantic import BaseModel
177	
178	class RefundHandoff(BaseModel):
179	    order_id: str
180	    customer_id: str
181	    reason: str
182	    verified: bool
183	    prior_contacts: list[str]
184	```
185	
186	When `handoff_to_refund_specialist(data: RefundHandoff)` is called, the runner validates the payload, rejects it if malformed, and passes the typed object into the refund agent's context. The refund agent knows exactly what it received — no prompt parsing, no ambiguity.
187	
188	Benefits:
189	
190	1. **Contract enforcement** — both ends agree on the shape
191	2. **Observability** — typed fields land cleanly in traces and dashboards
192	3. **Testability** — fixtures can drive individual agents with known payloads
193	4. **Versioning** — schema evolution is explicit (`v1 → v2` with migration rules)
194	
195	Typed schemas integrate with [[Agent Capability Negotiation and Binding]] — an agent advertises which handoff types it accepts, and orchestrators can wire only compatible agents. For [[Multi-Agent Memory Consistency]], typed schemas anchor the version and ownership fields that consistency protocols need.
196	
197	Cross-vendor typed handoffs should follow one of two emerging conventions: either a JSON-Schema payload negotiated during capability discovery, or a JSON-LD document with an agreed `@context` URL. JSON-Schema wins on familiarity and tooling; JSON-LD wins on semantic precision and is used by [[Agent Network Protocol]] and parts of [[Agent Discovery and Registries]] for capability descriptions. Either way, the payload is validated at the handoff boundary — the receiver refuses ill-formed handoffs before reasoning, instead of failing mid-task.
198	
199	A common anti-pattern is to pack structured data as a JSON-stringified blob inside a plain message. The receiver then has to re-parse prose-like output, guessing at field names. Typed handoffs kill this category of bug outright: the field is `order_id`, not "the order number was 1234 I think."
200	
201	## Conversation History Trimming
202	
203	Full-history handoffs grow unboundedly. After ten handoffs in a long session, the history may exceed the context window or consume most of the budget. Trimming strategies:
204	
205	- **Window** — keep the last N messages plus the initial user turn
206	- **Summarize-and-drop** — after threshold, replace older messages with a running summary
207	- **Hierarchical** — maintain a summary per-handoff-segment, keep full detail only within the current segment
208	- **Importance-weighted** — score messages (user turns, tool results with high entropy, handoff events) and drop low-scoring ones
209	
210	The OpenAI Agents SDK's sessions layer handles this via managed memory — history persists across interactions but is compressed strategically. LangGraph's state graph allows per-node trimming: the supervisor keeps full history, each worker sees only its briefing.
211	
212	Trimming is a consistency hazard. If an agent receives a trimmed history that drops a key fact ("user has premium tier"), it will respond as if the fact was never stated. Trimming decisions must be encoded in the handoff schema so receivers know what was elided — a summary marker, a dropped-fields list, a pointer to the canonical store.
213	
214	A robust pattern: **pin-and-trim**. Critical facts (user tier, verified email, payment authorization) are pinned into a structured facts record that survives all trimming. Non-critical history (casual prose, filler turns) gets trimmed aggressively. The receiver always has the pinned facts regardless of history depth. This models the distinction between **working memory** (can be lossy) and **authoritative state** (must be preserved) that [[Multi-Agent Memory Consistency]] formalizes.
215	
216	When a handoff crosses a process boundary, trimming must also respect encoding. Some agents speak text-only; others accept tool-call records, system metadata, or vision inputs. Transferring a history full of binary image data to a text-only agent wastes tokens and muddles reasoning. Handoff schemas should declare the receiver's supported modalities so the sender strips incompatible content at the boundary.
217	
218	## Loss and Telephone-Game Effects
219	
220	The [[langchain-benchmarking-multi-agent-architectures|LangChain benchmark]] quantified handoff degradation. The initial supervisor architecture performed poorly; three modifications recovered ~50% of performance:
221	
222	1. **Removing handoff messages from worker context** — workers no longer saw the supervisor's routing commentary
223	2. **Forwarding tool** — supervisor forwards worker output verbatim instead of paraphrasing
224	3. **Tool naming optimization** — `transfer_to` vs `delegate_to` changed handoff reliability
225	
226	The diagnosis: "translation mistakes" are the supervisor's primary failure mode. Whenever a handoff re-expresses information, fidelity drops. This mirrors the child's "telephone" game — each retransmission introduces noise, and by the fourth hop, the original message is unrecognizable.
227	
228	Concrete anti-patterns:
229	
230	- **Supervisor summarizes worker output** → detail lost, worker's reasoning obscured
231	- **Worker summarizes user intent before responding** → user sees restated version, feels misheard
232	- **Each agent re-asks clarifying questions** → context from prior handoff not consumed
233	- **Hardcoded handoff flows** → agents don't adapt when state violates assumptions
234	
235	The [[multi-agent-memory-computer-architecture|MAS memory position paper]] explains this through consistency: when handoffs don't preserve causal ordering of writes, the receiver reasons from incomplete history. Solutions map directly:
236	
237	- **Forward raw artifacts** — link, don't summarize; the worker's output is an artifact the supervisor references
238	- **Shared state graph** — both agents read from the same state; no transfer copying
239	- **Typed schemas** — what gets transferred is explicit, auditable, testable
240	
241	## Handoff Observability
242	
243	Handoffs are the highest-value spans to instrument. A handoff trace should capture:
244	
245	- **Source agent** identity and version
246	- **Target agent** identity and version
247	- **Payload** (full, typed)
248	- **Trigger** (which tool call initiated the handoff, with arguments)
249	- **History depth** at the moment of transfer
250	- **Timing** — time-to-handoff from user input, handoff latency itself
251	
252	With W3C `traceparent` propagation, a multi-agent trace shows handoffs as span transitions with a clear parent-child relationship — see [[Agent Observability and Distributed Tracing]] for the OpenTelemetry semantics. LangSmith traces show the full execution tree: every LLM call, tool invocation, handoff, and intermediate reasoning.
253	
254	Production-grade debugging questions to answer from traces:
255	
256	1. **Handoff fanout** — how many handoffs per session on average? Outliers?
257	2. **Return-to-sender** — are handoffs bouncing back (A → B → A)?
258	3. **Dead-end handoffs** — does a target agent fail immediately after receiving a handoff?
259	4. **Latency per agent** — which agent is slow after handoff?
260	5. **Payload drift** — does payload size grow across handoffs?
261	
262	[[Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns]] discusses broader orchestration visibility; handoff-specific observability is a subset focused on transfer boundaries.
263	
264	## Testing Handoff Correctness
265	
266	Handoff tests live at three layers:
267	
268	### Unit (per agent, with handoff fixtures)
269	
270	Give the receiver agent a canned handoff payload plus a history; assert the receiver's first action. Verifies the agent understands the handoff contract.
271	
272	```python
273	def test_refund_agent_handles_handoff():
274	    history = [user_msg("I want a refund"), triage_msg("Handing to refund")]
275	    payload = RefundHandoff(order_id="1234", customer_id="u-7", reason="damaged", verified=True, prior_contacts=[])
276	    result = refund_agent.run(history=history, handoff_payload=payload)
277	    assert result.tool_calls[0].name == "issue_refund"
278	```
279	
280	### Integration (end-to-end with mocked LLM)
281	
282	Replay a recorded user conversation through the full orchestrator; assert which agents were invoked in which order. Verifies handoff triggers fire correctly.
283	
284	### Regression (production trace replay)
285	
286	Capture production traces that exhibit issues; rerun them against the updated system; diff outcomes. [[Agent Observability and Distributed Tracing|LangSmith-style replay]] is the standard tool here.
287	
288	Critical handoff tests:
289	
290	- **Round-trip preservation** — given a handoff and a handoff-back, does state match?
291	- **Schema negative tests** — does a malformed payload get rejected before reaching the target agent?
292	- **Trim-safe** — if history is trimmed at the handoff, does the receiver still complete the task?
293	- **Cycle detection** — does an A→B→A→B loop terminate within max turns?
294	
295	## Patterns and Anti-Patterns
296	
297	Patterns that work:
298	
299	- **Schema-typed payload + full history** — best of both worlds; types for critical facts, history for nuance
300	- **Forwarding tool on supervisor** — supervisor passes worker output verbatim via a `forward_tool_result` tool
301	- **Handoff-back tool on workers** — every worker has an explicit `handoff_to_supervisor` to re-enter the supervisor context
302	- **Barrier before transfer** — flush pending writes before the handoff (see [[Multi-Agent Memory Consistency]] on handoff visibility ordering)
303	- **Capability pre-check** — supervisor verifies the target agent's capabilities before handoff (see [[Agent Capability Negotiation and Binding]])
304	
305	Anti-patterns to avoid:
306	
307	- **Supervisor paraphrasing worker output** — telephone-game degradation
308	- **Workers unaware of each other** in swarm pattern with >5 agents — handoff graph becomes unmanageable
309	- **Untyped string payloads** — receivers parse prompts, brittleness compounds
310	- **History-only transfer** — critical facts get trimmed, receiver operates blind
311	- **No observability** — swarm without [[Agent Observability and Distributed Tracing|distributed tracing]] is impossible to debug
312	
313	The guiding heuristic: treat each handoff as a remote procedure call with a versioned interface. Handoffs that degenerate into "vibe-based" transfers — where the receiver infers intent from prose — accumulate errors. Handoffs that transfer typed payloads, preserve raw artifacts, and emit rich traces compose into reliable multi-agent systems.

30	
31	# Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns
32	
33	Multi-agent systems emerge when a single agent can no longer cover a problem's surface area — context windows saturate, specializations diverge, or different teams own different capabilities. This article catalogs the canonical orchestration patterns — supervisor, swarm, hierarchical, sequential, concurrent, handoff, group chat, and Magentic — documents when each is appropriate, and covers the observability scaffolding required to run them in production. Published guidance converges on a consistent principle: **start with one agent; adopt multi-agent patterns only when the single-agent limit is empirically reached.**
34	
35	## When Multi-Agent Becomes Necessary
36	
37	Microsoft's Azure Architecture Center stages complexity across three levels:
38	
39	| Level | Description | Use when |
40	|-------|-------------|----------|
41	| Direct model call | Single LLM call + prompt, no tools | Classification, summarization, single-step tasks |
42	| Single agent with tools | One agent loops through tools and reasoning | Varied queries in one domain, most enterprise use cases |
43	| Multi-agent orchestration | Multiple specialized agents coordinate | Cross-domain problems, security boundaries per agent, parallel specialization |
44	
45	LangChain's guidance is identical: "Start with a single agent and good prompt engineering. Add tools before adding agents." Anthropic research cited by LangChain shows a multi-agent architecture (Claude Opus 4 + Claude Sonnet 4 subagents) outperforming single-agent Claude Opus 4 by 90.2% on research evaluations — but only on research tasks where genuine specialization exists. CrewAI, operating on 1.7 billion processed workflows, frames the same issue as architectural rather than intelligence-bound: "The gap isn't intelligence. It's architecture."
46	
47	Two constraints drive the transition:
48	
49	1. **Context management** — specialized knowledge for each capability exceeds what fits in a single prompt
50	2. **Distributed development** — different teams own different capabilities, and monolithic prompts become unmaintainable
51	
52	## Topology Taxonomy
53	
54	The field uses overlapping names for the same shapes. This table consolidates terminology across Azure, LangChain, LangGraph, Kore.ai, OpenAI, and CrewAI:
55	
56	| Shape | Canonical names | Who decides next step |
57	|-------|-----------------|----------------------|
58	| Linear | Sequential, pipeline, prompt chaining | Fixed order |
59	| Parallel | Concurrent, fan-out/fan-in, scatter-gather, router | Fixed or routed |
60	| Centralized | Supervisor, orchestrator-worker, subagents, hierarchical (1 level) | Supervisor |
61	| Nested centralized | Hierarchical teams, sub-crews | Supervisor of supervisors |
62	| Conversational | Group chat, roundtable, debate, council | Chat manager |
63	| Decentralized | Swarm, adaptive network, handoff mesh | Any agent |
64	| Adaptive | Magentic, task-ledger orchestration | Manager agent + plan |
65	
66	```
67	Sequential:    A → B → C → D
68	
69	Concurrent:    ┌→ A ─┐
70	               R───→ B ─┼→ Merge
71	               └→ C ─┘
72	
73	Supervisor:         ┌──────┐
74	                    │ Sup  │
75	                    └──┬───┘
76	                  ┌────┼────┐
77	                  ▼    ▼    ▼
78	                  A    B    C
79	
80	Hierarchical:      ┌ Top Sup ┐
81	                  ▼           ▼
82	              ┌ Sub Sup ┐  ┌ Sub Sup ┐
83	              ▼   ▼    ▼   ▼   ▼    ▼
84	              A1  A2  A3   B1  B2   B3
85	
86	Swarm:         A ←→ B
87	                ╲  ╱
88	                 ╳
89	                ╱  ╲
90	               C ←→ D
91	```
92	
93	## Supervisor Pattern
94	
95	A central orchestrator agent receives a request, decomposes it into subtasks, delegates to specialized worker agents (via tool calls or handoffs), synthesizes their outputs, and responds to the user. This is the **orchestrator-worker** pattern and the most common production default.
96	
97	### Responsibilities
98	
99	- Task decomposition and planning
100	- Delegation to worker agents
101	- Output validation and synthesis
102	- Progress monitoring and error recovery
103	- Compliance logging
104	
105	### LangGraph Implementation Sketch
106	
107	```python
108	from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
109	
110	def supervisor(state):
111	    next_agent = decide_next(state["messages"])  # LLM call
112	    return {"next": next_agent}
113	
114	def loan_agent(state): ...
115	def transaction_agent(state): ...
116	def payment_agent(state): ...
117	
118	graph = StateGraph(State)
119	graph.add_node("supervisor", supervisor)
120	graph.add_node("loan", loan_agent)
121	graph.add_node("transaction", transaction_agent)
122	graph.add_node("payment", payment_agent)
123	graph.add_conditional_edges("supervisor", lambda s: s["next"])
124	graph.add_edge("loan", "supervisor")
125	graph.add_edge("transaction", "supervisor")
126	graph.add_edge("payment", "supervisor")
127	```
128	
129	Kore.ai's banking example: "Pay off my car loan using my savings account" → supervisor routes to Loan Agent, Transaction Manager, Payment Processor in parallel where possible, validates consistency, logs transactions for compliance.
130	
131	A concrete instance of this topology is [[claude-code/wiki/concepts/Agent Swarm and Subagents|Claude Code's Agent Swarm]], where a **Leader** agent orchestrates specialized **Teammate** subagents through a file-based mailbox. The Leader's three execution models (fork, teammate, worktree) map onto this pattern: fork maximizes prompt-cache sharing across cheap parallel workers, teammate gives each worker an independent process, and worktree isolates filesystem side effects — all under a single hierarchical orchestrator. For comparison, [[openclaw/wiki/concepts/ACP and Sub-Agents|OpenClaw's ACP and Sub-Agents]] implements multi-agent orchestration with ACP harness for external CLI tools and session delegation, while [[goclaw/wiki/concepts/Agent Teams and Delegation|GoClaw's Agent Teams]] provides permission-gated delegation with team task boards and Lead/Member roles.
132	
133	### Supervisor Translation Overhead
134	
135	LangChain's benchmarking found supervisors underperform on first implementation due to **translation mistakes** — information loss as sub-agent responses flow through the supervisor. Three modifications yielded ~50% performance gains:
136	
137	1. **Remove handoff messages from sub-agent context** — workers do not see routing logic
138	2. **Forwarding messages tool** — supervisor forwards sub-agent responses verbatim, not paraphrased
139	3. **Tool naming optimization** — "delegate_to" vs "transfer_to" phrasing matters
140	
141	### Use When
142	
143	- Complex multi-domain workflows with visible reasoning requirements
144	- Text-based or asynchronous applications
145	- Compliance needs transparent traces
146	- Workflow must be predictable
147	
148	### Avoid When
149	
150	- Real-time / voice systems (supervisor adds hops)
151	- Strict token budgets (central synthesis inflates tokens)
152	- Very high scale (supervisor bottlenecks)
153	
154	## Swarm Pattern
155	
156	Multiple specialized agents aware of each other, transferring control directly via handoffs — no central supervisor. Only one agent is active at a time; responses go directly to users.
157	
158	### LangChain Benchmarking Results
159	
160	Using a modified τ-bench with six synthetic distractor domains (home improvement, tech support, pharmacy, automotive, restaurant, Spotify management, each with 19 tools), LangChain compared single-agent, swarm, and supervisor architectures:
161	
162	- **Single agent** — excels with few distractors, degrades sharply as irrelevant context grows
163	- **Swarm** — slightly outperforms supervisor; flat token usage as distractors scale
164	- **Supervisor** — higher token use than swarm due to translation overhead
165	
166	The swarm wins on both performance and token efficiency once you exceed two or three domains.
167	
168	### Swarm Limitations
169	
170	- **Every agent must know every peer** — not feasible across organizational boundaries or with third-party agents
171	- **Complex handoff graphs break** — "why did the user end up at Agent F instead of Agent D?" is hard to answer without distributed tracing
172	- **No single responder** — debugging is non-local
173	
174	LangGraph's official warning: *"If you don't have distributed tracing, don't use this pattern."* See the Observability section below.
175	
176	### OpenAI Agents SDK: Handoffs as Primitives
177	
178	OpenAI's Agents SDK (production successor to Swarm, shipped March 2026) treats handoffs as a first-class primitive. A handoff is simply a tool call that returns another Agent; the runner switches the active agent while preserving the shared conversation history.
179	
180	```python
181	from agents import Agent, Runner
182	
183	triage = Agent(
184	    name="Triage",
185	    instructions="Route user to the right specialist",
186	    handoffs=[technical_agent, billing_agent, refund_agent],
187	)
188	
189	result = Runner.run_sync(triage, "My payment was charged twice")
190	# active agent switches to billing_agent mid-conversation
191	```
192	
193	Agent instructions become the system prompt of the conversation. When a handoff fires, the system prompt changes but chat history persists — like a phone transfer where the next person has the full context.
194	
195	### Use When
196	
197	- Optimal agent not known upfront
198	- Domain expertise emerges during processing
199	- Many specialized domains, one active at a time
200	
201	### Avoid When
202	
203	- Parallel coordination across domains needed
204	- Task ownership is ambiguous
205	- Third-party agents in the mix (they cannot learn peer graph)
206	
207	## Hierarchical Pattern
208	
209	Supervisors of supervisors. LangGraph's "hierarchical agent teams" pattern nests sub-graphs, each with its own supervisor and specialist workers. A top-level supervisor routes between **teams**; each team handles its own internal delegation.
210	
211	```
212	         Top Supervisor
213	         ╱           ╲
214	    Research Team   Writing Team
215	    ╱    |    ╲     ╱    |    ╲
216	  Web  Docs  RAG   Draft Edit Fact
217	```
218	
219	Hierarchy is the dominant pattern once the number of specialists exceeds the cognitive capacity of a single supervisor's prompt. CrewAI's process model supports this directly: Crews contain Agents; Flows orchestrate multiple Crews.
220	
221	## Sequential Pattern
222	
223	Agents chained in a fixed linear order — a pipeline. Each agent processes the previous output and emits to the next.
224	
225	```
226	Input → Template Select → Clause Customize → Compliance → Risk → Output
227	```
228	
229	Azure's canonical example is a law firm contract pipeline: template selection → clause customization → regulatory compliance → risk assessment. Each stage has a clear dependency on the previous.
230	
231	**Use when** dependencies are strictly ordered, for data transformation pipelines, and for progressive refinement (draft → review → polish).
232	
233	**Avoid when** stages are embarrassingly parallel, when workflow requires backtracking, or when early-stage failures poison downstream agents.
234	
235	## Concurrent Pattern
236	
237	Multiple agents run in parallel on the same input, producing independent perspectives that are merged at the end. Also called fan-out/fan-in, scatter-gather, map-reduce, or ensemble reasoning.
238	
239	```
240	                ┌→ Fundamental  ─┐
241	                ├→ Technical    ─┤
242	     Input ────→├→ Sentiment    ─┼→ Synthesizer → Output
243	                ├→ ESG          ─┤
244	                └→ Macro        ─┘
245	```
246	
247	Azure's example: financial stock analysis with fundamental, technical, sentiment, and ESG agents operating on the same ticker in parallel. Latency is bounded by the slowest agent rather than the sum of all agents.
248	
249	**Use when** tasks are independent, when multiple perspectives strengthen output (ensemble, quorum/voting), and when time-sensitivity demands parallelism.
250	
251	**Avoid when** agents must build on each other's work, when deterministic ordering matters, or when no clear conflict-resolution strategy exists.
252	
253	## Handoff Pattern
254	
255	Dynamic delegation where agents themselves decide whether to handle a task or transfer to a more appropriate specialist. The handoff primitive is shared between OpenAI Agents SDK, LangGraph swarms, and several production frameworks.
256	
257	Azure's telecom CRM example:
258	
259	```
260	User: "I can't log in and my bill is wrong"
261	  ↓
262	Triage Agent → Account Access Agent (password reset)
263	            ↓
264	            Financial Resolution Agent (bill correction)
265	            ↓
266	            Human Support Agent (escalation)
267	```
268	
269	LangChain distinguishes handoffs from supervisor delegation: in handoffs, state persists across transitions and each agent transfers to another via tool calling. In supervisor, the supervisor retains control and workers are stateless.
270	
271	**Guardrails required**: max_turns caps to prevent infinite handoff loops; approval gates before state-changing actions; server-side argument validation; tool allowlists.
272	
273	## Group Chat Pattern
274	
275	Multiple agents participate in a shared conversation thread. A **chat manager** decides turn order. Also called roundtable, collaborative, multi-agent debate, or council.
276	
277	### Maker-Checker Loop
278	
279	A common sub-pattern: one agent produces content, another evaluates it against defined criteria, the cycle repeats until approval or a max-iterations cap. Also called evaluator-optimizer, generator-verifier, critic loop.
280	
281	LangGraph's **multi-agent collaboration** variant shares a common scratchpad of messages across agents. A rule-based router directs flow based on tool invocations or a "FINAL ANSWER" signal. Microsoft's guidance: limit to **three or fewer** agents to maintain control — group chat loops escalate quickly.
282	
283	**Use when** creative brainstorming, maker-checker quality review, consensus-building matters.
284	
285	**Avoid when** basic delegation suffices, real-time latency matters, or the chat manager has no way to determine task completion.
286	
287	## Magentic Pattern
288	
289	Designed for open-ended problems with no predetermined solution path. A **manager agent** builds and refines a **task ledger** through collaboration with specialized agents — iterating, backtracking, and re-delegating as needed.
290	
291	```
292	Manager Agent
293	├─ Task Ledger: [plan, sub-tasks, status, findings]
294	├─ Observes progress → updates ledger
295	├─ Detects stalls → backtracks and re-plans
296	└─ Delegates to specialists when plan requires
297	```
298	
299	Azure's example: SRE incident response — diagnostics, infrastructure, rollback, and communication agents coordinate via a manager building a dynamic task ledger.
300	
301	**Use when** the solution path is unknown, multiple specialists must collaborate adaptively, and a human-reviewable plan is required.
302	
303	**Avoid when** deterministic paths suffice, work is time-sensitive, or the task is low-complexity (manager overhead dominates).
304	
305	## Subagents, Skills, and Router (LangChain Framing)
306	
307	LangChain's article "Choosing the Right Multi-Agent Architecture" classifies four core patterns distinct from the topology taxonomy:
308	
309	| Pattern | Description | Best for |
310	|---------|-------------|----------|
311	| **Subagents** | Supervisor coordinates stateless subagents via tool calls | Multiple distinct domains, centralized control |
312	| **Skills** | Single agent loads specialized prompts and knowledge on-demand (progressive disclosure) | One agent with many specializations |
313	| **Handoffs** | Active agent changes dynamically; state persists | Sequential customer-support style workflows |
314	| **Router** | Classify input, dispatch to specialists in parallel, synthesize | Parallel queries across knowledge sources |
315	
316	Performance benchmarks:
317	
318	- Single requests: Skills, Handoffs, and Router take 3 model calls; Subagents take 4
319	- Repeat requests: Skills and Handoffs save 40-50% of calls on subsequent turns due to retained context
320	- Multi-domain queries: Subagents and Router run parallel (~9K tokens) vs Skills sequential (~15K tokens)
321	
322	## Memory and Consistency
323	
324	Multi-agent systems are distributed memory systems. The arXiv paper *Multi-Agent Memory from a Computer Architecture Perspective* frames the problem as a three-layer hierarchy:
325	
326	| Layer | Contents |
327	|-------|----------|
328	| I/O | Audio, text, images, network calls |
329	| Cache | Compressed context, recent tool calls, KV caches, embeddings |
330	| Memory | Dialogue histories, vector databases, graph databases |
331	
332	The core principle: "agent performance is an end-to-end data movement problem." Memory decisions bifurcate:
333	
334	- **Shared memory** — all agents access a common pool (vector store, document DB). Knowledge reuse, but requires coherence (stale reads, concurrent writes).
335	- **Distributed memory** — each agent owns local memory with selective sync. Isolation, but state divergence is common.
336	
337	Most real systems mix: local working memory + selectively shared artifacts. The paper identifies two unsolved protocol gaps:
338	
339	1. **Cache Sharing Protocol** — no standard for reusing one agent's cached results across peers
340	2. **Memory Access Control** — read-only vs read-write permissions, access granularity (document, chunk, record, trace segment), scope boundaries
341	
342	Consistency is the "largest conceptual gap": read-time conflict handling, update-time visibility, ordering of concurrent writes. The same problems surveyed in [[Distributed Systems Fundamentals]] reappear at the agent-memory layer, with CRDTs and consensus as the underlying primitives. See [[ai-harness/wiki/concepts/Memory Systems for Agents|Memory Systems for Agents]] for implementation patterns.
343	
344	## Observability and Tracing
345	
346	Multi-agent failures cascade. Without full-trace visibility across agent boundaries, debugging is impossible. LangChain's State of Agent Engineering report: 89% of organizations have implemented agent observability, 62% have step-level tracing.
347	
348	### What to Instrument
349	
350	- **LLM calls** — model, inputs, token counts, latency
351	- **Tool calls** — selection criteria, arguments, results, duration
352	- **Retrieval steps** — queries sent, documents returned
353	- **Reasoning transitions** — why the agent moved between steps
354	- **State changes** — memory reads/writes
355	- **Handoffs** — source agent, target agent, carried state
356	
357	### OpenTelemetry for Agent Tracing
358	
359	OpenTelemetry (OTel) provides the vendor-neutral substrate. It defines three primitives:
360	
361	| Primitive | Purpose |
362	|-----------|---------|
363	| **Log** | Timestamped message from a component |
364	| **Span** | Single unit of work with name, timestamps, attributes |
365	| **Trace** | Path of a request across spans and services |
366	
367	A span contains a name, time-range, structured logs, and attributes (metadata pairs):
368	
369	```
370	{
371	  "name": "agent.tool_call",
372	  "span_id": "c3fb4a...",
373	  "parent_span_id": "9a21f8...",
374	  "trace_id": "0af7651916cd43dd8448eb211c80319c",
375	  "attributes": {
376	    "agent.name": "billing_agent",
377	    "tool.name": "lookup_invoice",
378	    "tool.arguments": {...},
379	    "llm.model": "claude-sonnet-4",
380	    "llm.input_tokens": 1842,
381	    "llm.output_tokens": 216
382	  }
383	}
384	```
385	
386	**W3C Trace Context propagation** (`traceparent` header) carries trace IDs across service boundaries — essential when agents cross processes, containers, or network segments. OpenTelemetry defines five span kinds: Server (inbound), Client (outbound), Producer (async publish), Consumer (async process), Internal (in-process).
387	
388	### Waterfall View
389	
390	A trace visualized as a waterfall shows parent-child relationships across an entire agent workflow:
391	
392	```
393	Trace: user_request=plan_trip
394	├─ orchestrator (12.3s) ──────────────────────────────
395	│  ├─ llm.call (decompose) (1.2s) ─
396	│  ├─ handoff→flights_agent (3.4s)         ────
397	│  │  ├─ tool.search_flights (0.9s)         ──
398	│  │  └─ llm.call (synthesize) (0.8s)         ──
399	│  ├─ handoff→hotels_agent (2.8s)                 ───
400	│  │  ├─ tool.search_hotels (0.7s)                 ──
401	│  │  └─ llm.call (synthesize) (0.7s)                ──
402	│  └─ llm.call (final_answer) (2.1s)                   ──
403	```
404	
405	### Native SDKs vs OpenTelemetry
406	
407	| Option | Strength | Weakness |
408	|--------|----------|----------|
409	| LangSmith | Deep LangChain integration, @traceable decorator | Framework-tied |
410	| Laminar | Low overhead, vendor-neutral | Smaller ecosystem |
411	| Langfuse | Open-source, LLM-aware | ~15% overhead in 2026 benchmarks |
412	| AgentOps | Agent-specific metrics | ~12% overhead |
413	| OpenTelemetry | Standard, vendor-neutral, works everywhere | Manual setup |
414	
415	### Evaluation Approaches
416	
417	- **LLM-as-a-judge** — LLMs auto-grade thousands of historical runs against subjective criteria
418	- **Code evaluations** — programmatic checks for objective criteria
419	- **Multi-turn evaluations** — whether agents achieved goals across full conversations
420	- **Online evaluations** — sampled production traffic for real-time degradation detection
421	
422	### The Agent Improvement Loop
423	
424	```
425	  ┌────────────────────────────────┐
426	  │ Capture production traces      │
427	  └────────┬───────────────────────┘
428	           │

449	
450	## Production Safety Patterns
451	
452	Common risks in multi-agent deployments: prompt injection, tool overreach, stale context, runaway loops, accidental over-sharing. OpenAI's Agents SDK safety patterns:
453	
454	- **Narrow action agents** — put write tools behind dedicated agents with approval gates (`execute_tools=False`)
455	- **Server-side argument validation** — never trust tool arguments generated by an LLM
456	- **Tool allowlists** — restrict which agents can invoke which tools
457	- **Strict max_turns caps** — force termination on runaway loops
458	- **Human approval for state-changing actions** — persist state at HITL gates so orchestration becomes checkpointable
459	- **Content-safety guardrails at multiple points** — input, tool calls, tool responses, final output
460	
461	Treat all agent-facing text as public content, maintain one source of truth for policies, and require human approval for anything that mutates external state. See [[Agent Authentication and Zero Trust]] for the identity controls that complement these patterns.
462	
463	## Pattern Selection Matrix
464	
465	| Requirement | Pattern |
466	|-------------|---------|
467	| Steps have strict dependencies | Sequential |
468	| Independent perspectives on same input | Concurrent |
469	| Centralized oversight, auditable traces | Supervisor |
470	| Many specialists, one active at a time | Swarm / Handoff |
471	| Nested teams, > ~5 specialists | Hierarchical |
472	| Creative brainstorming, maker-checker | Group chat (≤3 agents) |
473	| Open-ended, no predetermined path | Magentic |
474	| Distinct verticals, parallel queries | Router |
475	| Single agent with many specializations | Skills (progressive disclosure) |
476	
477	### Cost Characteristics
478	
479	| Pattern | Token Behavior | Latency |
480	|---------|---------------|---------|
481	| Sequential | Accumulates, limited concurrency | Sum of stages |
482	| Concurrent | Spikes; N× parallel model calls | Slowest agent |
483	| Supervisor | Higher (translation overhead) | Depends on fan-out |
484	| Swarm | Flat across domains | Active agent only |
485	| Magentic | Most variable (manager iterates) | Unpredictable |
486	
487	### Combining Patterns
488	
489	Real deployments rarely use one pattern. Industry guidance: **start centralized, decentralize at concrete bottlenecks**. Hierarchical coordination at the top with swarm patterns at the leaf level dominates production. A workflow might open with sequential ingest, fan out to a concurrent analysis stage, then route to a supervisor that coordinates specialist swarms. See [[The A2A Protocol]], [[Agent Network Protocol]], and [[Agent-to-Agent Protocol Landscape]] for the transport protocols that carry these orchestrations across organizational boundaries.
490	
491	## Common Pitfalls
492	
493	- Adding agents without meaningful specialization
494	- Using deterministic patterns for non-deterministic workflows (and vice versa)
495	- Sharing mutable state between concurrent agents without coherence
496	- Unbounded tool-call loops (always set `max_turns`)
497	- Ignoring multi-hop latency
498	- Context window bloat as agents accumulate history — apply compaction between agents
499	- Building swarms without distributed tracing
500	- Squeezing too much responsibility into one agent
501	- Monolithic prompts that prevent testing individual agents
502	
503	## Connections
504	
505	- [[Distributed Systems Fundamentals]] — CAP, consistency, consensus underlie shared agent memory
506	- [[Agent-to-Agent Protocol Landscape]] — protocols that carry orchestration messages
507	- [[The A2A Protocol]] — Google's A2A for cross-agent coordination
508	- [[Agent Network Protocol]] — decentralized swarm-style topology
509	- [[Agent Identity and Verifiable Credentials]] — identity for agents in handoffs
510	- [[Agent Authentication and Zero Trust]] — guardrails for state-changing actions
511	- [[P2P Networks and Service Discovery]] — substrate for swarm topologies
512	- [[Real-Time Communication Patterns]] — streaming transports for agent output
513	- [[Agent Discovery and Registries]] — finding the right specialist at runtime
514	- [[The Open Agentic Web]] — cross-organizational orchestration context
515	- [[ai-harness/wiki/concepts/Agent Communication Protocols|Agent Communication Protocols]] — protocol-level framing
516	- [[ai-harness/wiki/concepts/Memory Systems for Agents|Memory Systems for Agents]] — memory substrate
517	- [[ai-harness/wiki/concepts/Model Context Protocol|Model Context Protocol]] — tool layer beneath orchestration
518	- [[hermes/wiki/concepts/Subagent Delegation|Hermes Subagent Delegation]] — `delegate_task` isolation and depth limits in a shipping harness
519	- [[pi-mono/wiki/concepts/Pi Agent Core Runtime|Pi Agent Core Runtime]] — minimal event-driven agent core for comparison with heavier orchestrators
520	
521	## Sources

524	- [[langchain-choosing-multi-agent-architecture]] — Subagents, Skills, Handoffs, Router decision framework
525	- [[langchain-benchmarking-multi-agent-architectures]] — Single/swarm/supervisor benchmarks, translation overhead
526	- [[langgraph-multi-agent-workflows]] — Collaboration, supervisor, hierarchical, swarm in LangGraph
527	- [[openai-agents-sdk-handoffs]] — Handoff primitive, guardrails, safety patterns
528	- [[crewai-agentic-systems-architecture]] — Crews + Flows, organizational architecture paradigm
529	- [[kore-ai-orchestration-patterns]] — Supervisor, adaptive network, custom pattern decision framework
